title: ReAct Agent Loop (Thought / Action / Observation)
tldr: Run a ReAct-style agent loop with explicit Thought → Action → Observation cycles. Includes hard iteration cap, tool-error recovery, and FinalAnswer guard.
full_prompt:
You are a ReAct agent. You answer the user's question by interleaving Thought (reasoning) and Action (tool calls), observing each result before continuing.

AVAILABLE TOOLS (you'll be told which apply):
{tool_descriptions}

FORMAT for every step:
Thought: <one sentence — what's the next subgoal?>
Action: <tool_name>(<args>)
(system fills in Observation)
...repeat...
When you have enough info:
Thought: I have what I need.
FinalAnswer: <answer the user's question, citing observations>

RULES:
1. Max {max_iter} iterations. If you hit it without an answer, output 'FinalAnswer: I couldn't determine X after {max_iter} steps. Best guess: Y because Z.'
2. Don't loop on the same Action with the same args — if a tool gave an empty result, try a different tool or refine the args.
3. If a tool errors, your next Thought addresses the error explicitly.
4. Never invent an Observation. Wait for the system to provide it.
5. FinalAnswer must reference at least one Observation as evidence; if you can't, say 'I cannot answer with available tools.'

Begin.

input_variables.name: user_question
input_variables.example: What's the current weather in Paris and is it suitable for outdoor running?

input_variables.name: tool_descriptions
input_variables.example:
weather(city) → returns temp_c, conditions, humidity
air_quality(city) → returns aqi

expected_output.sample:
Thought: I need weather data for Paris.
Action: weather('Paris')
Observation: {temp_c: 14, conditions: 'cloudy', humidity: 78}

Thought: Suitable for running depends also on air quality.
Action: air_quality('Paris')
Observation: {aqi: 42}

Thought: 14°C, cloudy, AQI 42 — comfortable + clean air.
FinalAnswer: Paris is currently 14°C, cloudy, with AQI 42 (good air quality). Suitable for outdoor running — light jacket recommended.

use_cases.example: Customer-support bot has tools for order lookup, refund, escalate; this loop drives the multi-step resolution.
Agent runs web_search + read_url + summarize tools to answer multi-hop questions.
Tools: check_status, restart_service, page_oncall; agent chooses sequence based on incident state.
Tools: read_file, write_file, run_test; agent fixes bugs by editing + testing.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for single-shot tasks where one tool call suffices. Also skip when latency matters more than reasoning (chat-completion mode is faster).

few_shot_examples.input: user_question: 'Did the deploy succeed?' tools: check_deploy_status(env) → {status, last_run_at}. max_iter: 4.
few_shot_examples.output:
Thought: I need the deploy status for the current env.
Action: check_deploy_status('production')
Observation: {status: 'failed', last_run_at: '2026-05-14T16:30Z'}

Thought: It failed; the user probably wants the failure reason.
Action: get_deploy_log('production', 'last')
Observation: 'Error: missing env var DATABASE_URL'

FinalAnswer: No — production deploy failed at 16:30 UTC. Cause: missing env var DATABASE_URL. Re-run after setting it.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at ReAct discipline — doesn't skip Thought, doesn't loop.
Strong; occasionally invents Observations — explicit rule needed.
Loops without strict iteration cap; enforce max_iter strictly.

variations.label: Plan-first
variations.description: Generate a plan before acting.
variations.prompt_snippet: Before first Thought, output 'Plan:' with 2-4 bullets of intended subgoals. Then execute Thought/Action loop.

variations.label: Reflection-step
variations.description: After every 3 iterations, reflect.
variations.prompt_snippet: Every 3 iterations, emit 'Reflection: <what's working, what's not, should we change approach?>'.

variations.label: Multi-agent
variations.description: Delegate to specialized sub-agents.
variations.prompt_snippet: Treat sub-agents as tools. Each has its own ReAct loop; this top-level agent orchestrates.

failure_modes.fix: Strict rule — wait for system to provide Observation; never fabricate

failure_modes.symptom: Loops on the same Action with no progress
failure_modes.fix: Rule 2 — refine args or switch tools after empty result
FinalAnswer must cite ≥1 Observation; otherwise 'cannot answer'

failure_modes.symptom: Skips Thought, goes straight to Action
failure_modes.fix: Every Action requires a Thought preceding it; reject malformed steps

faq.question: What's max_iter for production?
faq.answer: 5-8 for most tasks. >10 = either task is too complex or tools are wrong abstraction.

faq.question: Should I show the loop to users?
faq.answer: Stream Thoughts to power users (debugging value). For end users, show only FinalAnswer + 'thinking...' indicator.

faq.question: How do I handle tool errors?
faq.answer: Tool returns an error in Observation; the next Thought addresses it. The prompt's rule 3 enforces this.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: ReAct Agent Loop Prompt — Thought/Action/Observation
meta_description: Run a ReAct-style agent loop with strict format, max-iter cap, tool-error recovery, no-hallucination guards.

title: Self-Critique Loop (draft → critique → revise)
tldr: Three-pass loop: draft answer, critique it against explicit criteria, revise. Improves quality 20-40% on complex tasks at 3x token cost.
full_prompt:
You produce high-quality output via 3-pass self-critique. Each pass has a strict role.

INPUTS:
- task: what to produce
- quality_criteria: 3-5 explicit dimensions (e.g., 'factual accuracy', 'specificity', 'no hallucinated citations')

PASS 1 — DRAFT:
Produce the best answer you can in one shot. Don't second-guess; flow.

PASS 2 — CRITIQUE:
Review your own draft against each quality_criteria. For each criterion: 'Pass / Fail / Partial' with 1-2 sentences of evidence. List the top 3 specific issues to fix.

PASS 3 — REVISE:
Produce a revised version that addresses every issue from PASS 2. Don't add new issues. If a critique was wrong, note 'PASS 2 was incorrect: ...'.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
## Draft
[full draft]

## Critique
[criterion-by-criterion analysis]

## Revised
[final answer]

Begin.

input_variables.name: task
input_variables.description: The task to produce output for
input_variables.example: Write a 200-word product update announcing our new export feature

input_variables.name: quality_criteria
input_variables.example: ['No marketing jargon', 'Specific about the user benefit', 'Mention the technical limit', 'Under 200 words']

expected_output.sample:
## Draft
We're thrilled to announce... [marketing-ish 220 words]

## Critique
- No marketing jargon: Fail. 'Thrilled' is jargon.
- Specific benefit: Partial. Says 'faster exports' but no numbers.
- Technical limit: Fail. Doesn't mention the 10k-row cap.
- Under 200 words: Fail (220).

Issues: thrilled, no number, no cap.

## Revised
Exports are now 4x faster. New behavior: rows stream as they're generated; first results visible in <3s for tables up to 10,000 rows. Larger tables still batch — we're working on lifting the cap... [195 words]

use_cases.example: Blog post drafts; the critique catches what the first pass misses.
Engineering RFC; critique against 'clarity, completeness, falsifiability'.
Critique against 'no jargon, specific number, single call-to-action'.
Investor email or sensitive customer reply; revise pass tightens tone.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for short / simple outputs (1-line summaries) — overhead exceeds value. Also skip when latency matters more than quality.

few_shot_examples.input: task: 'Write a tweet announcing a 50% off sale this weekend'. criteria: ['Under 240 chars', 'Specific discount amount', 'Clear deadline', 'No hashtag spam']
few_shot_examples.output:
## Draft
🎉 BIG SAVINGS THIS WEEKEND! Don't miss out! #sale #deals #discount

## Critique
- Char count: Pass (~70).
- Specific discount: Fail. Doesn't say 50%.
- Clear deadline: Partial. 'This weekend' is vague.
- Hashtag spam: Fail. 3 hashtags = spammy.

## Revised
50% off, Friday 6pm to Sunday midnight (PT). No code needed. Link in bio.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at honest self-critique; doesn't sandbag the 'Fail' verdicts.
Use when stakes are high and depth matters.
Sometimes the critique is too lenient; instruct 'be harsh'.
Workable; revisions can be incremental rather than substantive.

variations.label: Adversarial critique
variations.description: Critique as a hostile reviewer.
variations.prompt_snippet: PASS 2 override: Adopt a hostile-reviewer persona. Find every possible issue. Steelman the worst-case critique.

variations.label: N-pass refinement
variations.description: Add a 4th and 5th pass for stubborn issues.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add PASS 4: critique the revision against the same criteria. If any still fail, PASS 5 revise. Cap at 5 passes.

variations.label: Two-model dialog
variations.description: Use a different model for critique vs draft.
variations.prompt_snippet: Note: this requires orchestration outside the prompt. Draft with Claude Sonnet 4.5; critique with GPT-5 (different blind spots); revise with Sonnet.

failure_modes.symptom: Critique is too gentle, all 'Pass' marks
failure_modes.fix: Instruct 'be harsh — find at least 1 issue per criterion'

failure_modes.symptom: Revision introduces new issues not flagged
failure_modes.fix: PASS 3 rule: 'don't add new issues'; if you do, flag them
If revision is <30% different from draft, it didn't actually address critiques; require visible rewrites

failure_modes.symptom: Loops on same issue across multiple passes
failure_modes.fix: Cap passes; if same issue persists after 3 revises, escalate to human

faq.question: How much does self-critique help?
faq.answer: 20-40% quality lift on complex tasks (long-form writing, code generation, multi-criteria outputs). Diminishing returns past 2 passes for simple tasks.

faq.question: Is it worth 3x cost?
faq.answer: For high-stakes one-shots: yes. For bulk runs: no — train a better single-pass prompt instead.

faq.question: Can I skip the draft and just critique?
faq.answer: No — the critique needs something concrete to react to. 'Critique without draft' produces vague generic advice.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Self-Critique Loop Prompt — Draft, Critique, Revise
meta_description: Three-pass quality improvement: draft, critique against explicit criteria, revise. 20-40% quality lift on long-form tasks.

title: RAG with Inline Citations + Confidence
tldr: Answer questions from retrieved context with sentence-level citations [doc:N]. Refuses to answer if context insufficient. Confidence score per claim.
full_prompt:
You answer questions from retrieved context. Every claim cites which retrieved chunk supports it. Refuse to answer if context is insufficient.

INPUTS:
- question: user's question
- retrieved_chunks: list of {chunk_id, source_doc, content, retrieval_score}
- refuse_threshold: float (default 0.6) — if best chunk score < threshold, refuse

PROCEDURE:
1. Check: do retrieved chunks contain enough info to answer?
   - If best chunk score < refuse_threshold OR question requires info not in any chunk → refuse.
2. Compose answer using ONLY content from retrieved chunks.
3. After every claim, insert [chunk_id] citing the chunk that supports it.
4. If a claim is supported by multiple chunks, cite all.
5. If parts of the question can be answered and parts cannot, answer the answerable parts; explicitly note what's not in the context.
6. End with a 'Confidence' line: 0.0-1.0 for the answer overall.

REFUSAL FORMAT:
'I can't answer this from the available sources. The retrieved documents cover [X, Y] but not [Z]. Try searching for: [suggestion].'

NEVER:
- Combine context info with your training data
- Cite a chunk you didn't use
- Fabricate chunk_ids

Begin.

input_variables.name: question
input_variables.example: What's our refund policy for annual plans?

input_variables.name: retrieved_chunks
input_variables.description: Retrieved chunks from your RAG retriever
input_variables.example: [{chunk_id:'c1', source_doc:'pricing.md', content:'Annual plans: refundable within 60 days...', retrieval_score:0.87}]

input_variables.name: refuse_threshold
input_variables.description: Minimum chunk score to attempt answer

expected_output.sample:
Annual plans are refundable within 60 days of purchase [c1]. After 60 days, refunds are at our discretion and require manager approval [c1]. Monthly plans follow a separate policy not covered in the retrieved docs.

Confidence: 0.85

use_cases.example: Employee asks 'what's our PTO policy?'; retriever pulls handbook chunks; this prompt answers with citations.
Customer asks question; retriever pulls KB articles; agent reviews cited answer before sending.
Regulatory questions answered against the policy library; citations let auditors trace claims.
'How do I use feature X?'; retrieve docs; answer with citations to the relevant doc sections.

when_not_to_use: Don't use when retrieval quality is poor (better to invest in retriever first). Also skip when answer needs reasoning beyond what's in chunks.

few_shot_examples.input: question: 'Does our app support SSO?'. chunks: [{c1: 'SAML SSO available on Enterprise plan. Setup via /security/sso.', score:0.91}, {c2: 'Free plan: email/password only.', score:0.78}]
few_shot_examples.output:
Yes — SAML SSO is available on the Enterprise plan and is configured at /security/sso [c1]. SSO is not available on the Free plan, which uses email/password only [c2].

Confidence: 0.90

model_compatibility.notes: Best at refusal discipline — won't fill gaps with training data.
Strong citations; sometimes leaks training data; explicit rule needed.
Workable; verify citations point to real content (not fabricated).

variations.label: Citation-format URL
variations.description: Citations as URLs not chunk_ids.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace [c1] with the source_doc URL. Useful when output goes to end users who need clickable refs.

variations.label: Multi-language answer
variations.description: Answer in user's language even if chunks are in another.
variations.prompt_snippet: Detect question language. Translate the answer; preserve citations as chunk_ids.

variations.label: Stricter refusal
variations.description: Tighten threshold; refuse more often.
variations.prompt_snippet: refuse_threshold: 0.8. Better to refuse than to answer marginally — preserves trust.

failure_modes.symptom: Combines retrieved context with training data
failure_modes.fix: Explicit rule + system reminder; if answer references X not in chunks, refuse

failure_modes.symptom: Cites chunks but answer doesn't actually use them
failure_modes.fix: Citations must be content-substantive, not decorative
Calibrate refuse_threshold; 0.6 default for most use cases
Verify chunk_ids exist in input before citing

faq.question: What if chunks contradict each other?
faq.answer: Output both with separate citations; flag the contradiction. Don't silently pick one.

faq.question: How many chunks should I retrieve?
faq.answer: 5-15 typically. <5 = low recall; >15 = context bloat + slower.

faq.question: Does confidence reflect retrieval quality?
faq.answer: Roughly — but it primarily reflects how well chunks answer the question. Re-rank chunks before this prompt for best results.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: RAG with Inline Citations + Refusal Guard
meta_description: Answer from retrieved chunks with per-claim citations, refuses on insufficient context, confidence score per answer.

title: Agentic RAG — Multi-Hop Research with Tool-Use
tldr: Multi-hop research agent: decompose question, retrieve+answer subquestions, synthesize. Handles 'compare X and Y across Z dimensions' style queries that simple RAG can't.
full_prompt:
You answer complex research questions that require multi-hop retrieval. Decompose first, retrieve per sub-question, synthesize.

INPUTS:
- question: user's complex question
- tools: retrieve(query, k=5), read_url(url), summarize(text), compare(items, dimensions)
- max_subquestions: cap (default 5)

PROCEDURE:
1. DECOMPOSE: break the question into 2-5 sub-questions. Each sub-question is answerable by retrieval.
2. For each sub-question, run retrieve(); if needed, follow up with read_url() on top results.
3. Answer each sub-question independently with citations.
4. SYNTHESIZE: combine sub-answers into the final answer. Surface contradictions across sources.
5. Confidence per claim + overall.

AVOID:
- Decomposing into >5 sub-questions (over-engineers simple questions)
- Running redundant retrievals (cache sub-answers)
- Synthesizing without citations from each sub-answer

Begin.

input_variables.name: question
input_variables.example: How do Pinecone and Qdrant compare on pricing, scaling limits, and SDK quality?

input_variables.name: tools
input_variables.example: ['retrieve', 'read_url', 'summarize']

expected_output.sample:
## Decomposition
1. Pinecone pricing tiers
2. Qdrant pricing tiers
3. Pinecone scaling limits
4. Qdrant scaling limits
5. SDK quality (both)

## Sub-answers
**1. Pinecone pricing:** Free tier 100k vectors; Standard $70/mo + usage [src1]. **2. Qdrant pricing:** Free Cloud 1GB; paid tier $50/mo + usage [src2]. ...

## Synthesis
Pinecone is more expensive at low volume but includes a managed serverless tier [src1]. Qdrant offers self-host as an option (Pinecone doesn't) [src2]. SDK quality is comparable; Qdrant has stronger TypeScript support [src3].

Confidence: 0.83

use_cases.example: Sales-engineering needs apples-to-apples comparison across 3-5 vendors.
Investor researching a stack: agent compares competitors on 5 dimensions.
Procurement team's vendor matrix is built from this prompt's output.
5-10 papers, 3-4 cross-cutting questions; produces comparative analysis.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for simple lookups (single retrieve+answer is enough). Also skip when there are no retrieval sources — different prompt territory.

few_shot_examples.input: question: 'Which is faster: pgvector with HNSW or Qdrant on 10M vectors?'. tools: retrieve, read_url. max_subqs: 3.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Decomposition
1. pgvector HNSW benchmarks at 10M vectors
2. Qdrant benchmarks at 10M vectors
3. Methodology comparison (apples to apples?)

## Sub-answers
**1. pgvector HNSW:** ~80ms p99 at 10M, single instance [src1]. **2. Qdrant:** ~12ms p99 at 10M [src2]. **3. Methodology:** src1 used m=16 ef=200; src2 used m=64 ef=512. Higher accuracy config in src2 actually disadvantages it but it still wins.

## Synthesis
Qdrant is 5-7x faster at 10M-vector scale based on these benchmarks. pgvector is simpler operationally but slower at scale.

Confidence: 0.75 (methodology differences could swing the comparison)

model_compatibility.notes: Best at decomposition discipline and contradiction-surfacing.

variations.label: Iterative-retrieval
variations.description: Each sub-answer can trigger follow-up retrievals.
variations.prompt_snippet: Allow sub-questions to spawn child sub-questions (max depth 2). Useful when initial retrieval reveals more questions.

variations.label: Cross-source-only
variations.description: Force comparison across 2+ sources per claim.
variations.prompt_snippet: Reject claims supported by only 1 source unless explicitly single-source-verifiable.

variations.label: Disagreement-focused
variations.description: Highlight where sources disagree.
variations.prompt_snippet: After synthesis, add 'Contradictions found' section listing every claim where 2+ sources disagreed + analysis of why.

failure_modes.symptom: Over-decomposes (5+ sub-Qs for a simple question)
failure_modes.fix: Cap at max_subquestions; merge sub-Qs that share answers
Synthesis must thread the citations from sub-answers; no new claims without sources
Disagreement-focused variation forces surfacing; otherwise weight the contradiction explicitly in confidence
Cache sub-answers; reject duplicate queries with empty results

faq.question: When is agentic RAG worth it vs simple RAG?
faq.answer: When the question needs 2+ retrievals to answer. Comparative questions, multi-entity questions, anything with 'and how does that compare to'.

faq.question: Latency impact?
faq.answer: 3-5x slower than single-shot RAG. Worth it for complex questions where simple RAG produces wrong/incomplete answers.

faq.question: Cost impact?
faq.answer: Roughly 5x. Each sub-question is its own retrieve+generate. Use cheaper model (Haiku) for sub-answers, Sonnet/Opus for synthesis.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Agentic RAG — Multi-Hop Research with Synthesis
meta_description: Decompose complex questions, retrieve per sub-question, synthesize with cross-source citations and contradiction-surfacing.

title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning (explicit steps)
tldr: Solve multi-step problems by working through explicit reasoning chains. Verifies each step before proceeding. Best for math, logic, multi-constraint problems.
full_prompt:
You solve problems by working through explicit reasoning chains. Don't skip steps; verify each before proceeding.

INPUTS:
- problem: the problem to solve
- domain: math | logic | code | mixed (helps calibrate verification)

STRUCTURE:
1. UNDERSTAND: restate the problem in your own words. Identify what's being asked + what's given.
2. PLAN: outline the approach in 2-4 steps. Don't commit to numbers yet.
3. EXECUTE: work through each step. After each, ask: 'does this make sense?' If not, back up.
4. VERIFY: check the answer against the problem. Plug it back in. Common-sense check.
5. ANSWER: state the final answer in 1 line.

RULES:
- One reasoning step per paragraph; never combine.
- If you spot an error mid-chain, stop and back up.
- Numbers: show arithmetic, don't just state results.
- Verify at the end — never skip the check.

Begin.

input_variables.name: problem
input_variables.example: A train leaves City A at 60mph at 2pm. Another leaves City B at 75mph at 3pm heading toward A. Cities are 300mi apart. When do they meet?

input_variables.name: domain
input_variables.example: math

expected_output.sample:
## Understand
Two trains heading toward each other. Train A: starts 2pm from City A at 60mph. Train B: starts 3pm from City B at 75mph. Cities 300mi apart. Find meet time.

## Plan
1. Train A travels alone from 2pm to 3pm — calculate distance covered.
2. From 3pm, both trains travel toward each other — calculate closing rate.
3. Find when they cover remaining distance together.

## Execute
Step 1: From 2pm to 3pm, Train A covers 60mph × 1hr = 60mi. Remaining distance = 300 - 60 = 240mi.
Step 2: From 3pm, closing speed = 60 + 75 = 135mph.
Step 3: Time to cover 240mi = 240 / 135 ≈ 1.778 hours = 1hr 47min.

## Verify
Meet at 3pm + 1hr 47min = 4:47pm. Train A traveled 2hr 47min × 60mph = 167mi. Train B traveled 1hr 47min × 75mph = 133mi. Total: 167 + 133 = 300mi ✓

## Answer
4:47pm

use_cases.example: Word problems where chain-of-thought halves error rate vs zero-shot.
Multi-constraint problems (zebra puzzles, scheduling).
Walking through suspect code paths to find the bug.
Weighing options with explicit pro/con chains.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for simple lookups or single-step tasks — overhead exceeds value. Also skip for tasks requiring creativity (CoT can flatten creative output).

few_shot_examples.input: problem: 'If a shirt was $40 with 20% discount, what's the final price after 8% tax?' domain: math

model_compatibility.notes: Workable for shallow chains; verify arithmetic.

variations.label: Tree-of-thought
variations.description: Explore multiple reasoning branches.
variations.prompt_snippet: Instead of one chain, generate 3 candidate approaches. Evaluate each; pick the most promising; continue from there.

variations.label: Self-consistency
variations.description: Generate 5 chains; majority-vote.
variations.prompt_snippet: Generate 5 independent chains. The final answer is whichever appears most often across chains. Useful for ambiguous problems.

variations.label: Verify-only
variations.description: Pre-existing answer; verify or debunk.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip Execute step. INPUT includes a proposed answer. Output: chain that either confirms or finds the flaw.

failure_modes.symptom: Skips verify step (math errors slip through)
failure_modes.fix: Verify is mandatory; plug answer back in
One step per paragraph rule; force explicitness
Show all arithmetic; for complex numbers, use a calculator tool if available

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't back up when error spotted
failure_modes.fix: If verify fails, RESTART from Plan with the issue noted

faq.question: When does CoT help vs hurt?
faq.answer: Helps: math, logic, multi-step decision. Hurts: creative writing (flattens), simple lookups (overhead).

faq.question: Should I show CoT to end users?
faq.answer: No — show the answer. CoT is for the model's reasoning, not the user's reading.

faq.question: Latency impact?
faq.answer: 2-3x slower than zero-shot. Worth it on problems where zero-shot accuracy is <80%.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompt — Explicit Reasoning with Verify
meta_description: Solve multi-step problems with explicit reasoning chains. Step-by-step structure, mandatory verify, math/logic/code domains.

title: A/B Test Result Interpreter (statistical + practical)
tldr: Given A/B test data (conversion rates, sample sizes, CI), produce plain-English interpretation, distinguish statistical vs practical significance, recommend ship/extend/kill.
full_prompt:
You interpret A/B test results for product/growth teams. Be rigorous about stats and brutal about practical significance — most 'wins' don't matter at the business level.

INPUTS:
- variants: list of {label, sample_size, conversion_count, conversion_rate}
- baseline_label: which variant is control
- primary_metric: name + unit (e.g., 'signup_rate', 'pp')
- guardrail_metrics (optional): secondary metrics with their deltas
- business_context: minimum lift threshold to bother shipping (e.g., '0.5pp absolute or 5% relative')
- test_duration_days, run_start_date, run_end_date

PROCEDURE:
1. Compute relative + absolute lift per variant vs baseline.
2. Compute z-test 2-proportion confidence interval at 95%. State CI bounds.
3. Distinguish statistical significance (CI excludes zero) from practical significance (lift > business threshold).
4. Check guardrails: any negative delta? Any sample-size imbalance >5%?
5. Recommend: SHIP / EXTEND / KILL / INCONCLUSIVE — with reasoning.
6. List confounders: novelty effect (was duration <14d?), seasonality, segment effects (if data provided).

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Headline (1 sentence), Numbers table, Significance verdict, Guardrail check, Recommendation, Confounders, Open questions.

Begin.

input_variables.name: variants
input_variables.example: [{label:'Control', sample_size:12450, conversion_count:1245, conversion_rate:10.0}, {label:'B-new-cta', sample_size:12380, conversion_count:1361, conversion_rate:11.0}]

input_variables.name: baseline_label
input_variables.example: Control

input_variables.name: primary_metric
input_variables.example: signup_rate (pp)

input_variables.name: business_context
input_variables.example: Min 5% relative lift to ship.

input_variables.name: guardrail_metrics
input_variables.example: [{name:'7-day-retention', control:0.62, variant:0.58, delta_significant:true}]

expected_output.sample:
## Headline
Variant B beat Control by +1.0pp absolute (10.0% lift), statistically significant, but watch the 7-day retention guardrail.

## Numbers
| Variant | N | Conversions | Rate | Δ abs | Δ rel | 95% CI (Δ abs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control | 12,450 | 1,245 | 10.0% | — | — | — |
| B-new-cta | 12,380 | 1,361 | 11.0% | +1.0pp | +10.0% | [+0.2pp, +1.8pp] |

## Significance
- Statistical: ✅ CI excludes 0
- Practical: ✅ +10% relative exceeds your 5% threshold

## Guardrails ⚠️
- 7-day retention dropped 0.62 → 0.58 (-4pp). Significant.

## Recommendation: EXTEND
The primary-metric win is real, but retention guardrail is concerning. Extend by 2 weeks to confirm retention isn't a novelty artifact. If retention recovers → SHIP. If sustained → KILL despite the conversion win.

use_cases.example: Product team's experiment review meeting; this prompt produces the v1 decision memo for each test.
PM proposes ship/kill; prompt's recommendation is the check. Diff is the learning.
Email subject-line test results; quick verdict on whether to scale.
Long-running pricing test; the prompt distinguishes novelty effect from durable lift.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for multi-armed bandits or sequential testing — those need different stats. Also skip when sample size <500 per arm; results are too noisy.

few_shot_examples.input: variants: Control 8500/850 (10.0%), B 8200/873 (10.6%). Min lift: 5% rel. Duration: 7 days. No guardrail signals.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Headline
Variant B beat Control by +0.6pp absolute (+6% relative); statistically marginal, practically thin.

## Numbers
| Variant | N | Conv | Rate | Δ abs | Δ rel | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control | 8,500 | 850 | 10.0% | — | — | — |
| B | 8,200 | 873 | 10.6% | +0.6pp | +6.0% | [-0.1pp, +1.3pp] |

## Significance
- Statistical: ⚠️ CI includes 0 (just barely)
- Practical: marginal (+6% just clears your 5% threshold)

## Recommendation: EXTEND
7 days is too short — likely novelty effect. Extend 2 weeks and re-evaluate.

## Confounders
- Test duration 7 days is below the 14-day novelty-effect threshold.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the statistical vs practical distinction.
Cheap for batch interpretation of many tests.
Stats can drift; verify CI manually.

variations.label: Bayesian framing
variations.description: Use posterior probability instead of frequentist CI.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace CI with 'P(B > Control) = X%' framing. Use Beta priors with sample = pseudo-counts (5,5).

variations.label: Segment-aware
variations.description: Drill into segment-level lift.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_breakdown (variant × segment matrix). Output per-segment lift; flag Simpson's paradox if overall lift sign differs from segment-level signs.

variations.label: Multiple-test correction
variations.description: When running 5+ tests in parallel.
variations.prompt_snippet: Apply Bonferroni or BH correction. Reduce effective alpha to 0.05/n_tests; recompute CI bounds.

failure_modes.symptom: Conflates statistical significance with practical significance
failure_modes.fix: Always report both separately; require lift exceeds business_context threshold for 'SHIP' recommendation
Hard rule: any significant guardrail regression → recommendation is EXTEND minimum, not SHIP
Check N delta; if >5%, flag 'assignment imbalance — debug before trusting results'

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't account for short-duration novelty effect
failure_modes.fix: Flag tests <14 days as 'novelty risk' in Confounders

faq.question: What's a 'practical significance' threshold?
faq.answer: Depends on the metric. For top-of-funnel: 5-10% relative lift. For revenue: 1-2% can matter at scale. Set it in business_context.

faq.question: How do I handle ties?
faq.answer: If CI includes 0 and lift < 1.5× business threshold: INCONCLUSIVE. Re-run with more data or kill.

faq.question: Can it do sequential / Bayesian?
faq.answer: Frequentist by default. Use the Bayesian-framing variation for that style.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: A/B Test Result Interpreter — Statistical + Practical
meta_description: Interpret A/B test results with CI, guardrail checks, novelty-effect flags, and SHIP/EXTEND/KILL recommendation.

title: Cohort Retention Analyzer
tldr: Given cohort retention data (signup-month × week-N), find the inflection point, classify retention curve shape (smiling/flat/leaky), and recommend the next intervention.
full_prompt:
You analyze cohort retention tables for product/growth teams. Cohort retention = % of users from signup-cohort N still active in week K.

INPUTS:
- cohort_table: 2D array — cohort_label (signup month) × week_number → retention %
- product_type: 'transactional' | 'habit-forming' | 'b2b-tool' | 'social'
- benchmark_target_w8_retention (optional): your target W8 retention %
- product_changes: list of changes by date (helps explain cohort shifts)

PROCEDURE:
1. For each cohort, identify the W1, W4, W8, W12 retention values.
2. Classify the curve shape:
   - SMILING: drops then rises (sign of habit formation)
   - FLAT: stabilizes after initial drop (sticky)
   - LEAKY: declines continuously
   - CLIFF: sharp drop at specific week (e.g., trial expiry)
3. Compare cohorts: is later-cohort retention better/worse/same?
4. Cross-reference product_changes — did a known change correlate with a cohort shift?
5. Recommend the highest-leverage intervention: onboarding fix? activation moment? feature launch?

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with shape verdict, cohort comparison, intervention recommendation, what data to gather next.

Begin.

input_variables.name: product_type
input_variables.example: habit-forming

input_variables.name: product_changes
input_variables.example: [{date:'2026-02-15', change:'shipped self-serve onboarding'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Curve shape: SMILING for Feb+ cohorts

Feb cohort: 70→52→41→35→33→32→**31% at W8**, then rising to 33% at W12. Classic smiling curve — habit formation kicking in by W10.

## Cohort comparison
- Jan: W8 retention 29% (LEAKY shape)
- Feb: W8 retention 31% (SMILING shape — first cohort to show it)
- Mar: W8 retention 34% (SMILING, steeper)

The Feb 15 onboarding ship correlates with the shape change. Likely causal.

## Recommendation
Double down on activation. Mar cohort suggests the onboarding ship is driving real retention. Next intervention: identify the activation event that distinguishes Mar-cohort survivors from churners — likely a habit-forming action in W1-W3.

use_cases.example: First Monday of every month, run on the prior month's cohort table; the recommendation goes into the team's growth review.
Big feature shipped 2 months ago — did the cohort that experienced it from day 1 retain better than older cohorts?
After an onboarding A/B test ends, follow up at W8 to see if the conversion lift translated to retention lift.
Retention is dropping; this prompt helps distinguish 'we're acquiring worse users' from 'the product is leaking value'.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for transactional / one-time-purchase products — retention curves there are meaningless. Also skip if cohorts have <500 users (noise dominates).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 cohorts (Jan/Feb/Mar 2026), each 8 weeks. W8 retention: 22%/24%/23%. product_type: b2b-tool. Changes: 'Feb 1: new pricing tier launched'.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Curve shape: FLAT across all cohorts (mid-20s W8 retention)

No material change between Jan, Feb, Mar. New pricing tier (Feb 1) didn't move retention — it may have affected acquisition mix, not value capture.

## Cohort comparison
W1: 65% / 67% / 64% — basically flat
W4: 38% / 39% / 37% — flat
W8: 22% / 24% / 23% — flat

## Recommendation
Retention is the bottleneck, not pricing. For a B2B tool, 23% W8 is below typical benchmark (35-50%). Focus on:
1. Identify the 'aha' moment — what does the 23% surviving cohort have in common in W1?
2. Get more users to that moment within first 7 days.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at distinguishing flat-but-fine from flat-and-bad.
Preferred when 10+ cohorts are in scope.

variations.label: Segmented cohort
variations.description: Split by user segment (free vs paid, plan size, etc.).
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_cohort_tables = dict of {segment_name: cohort_table}. Output per-segment shape + cross-segment comparison.

variations.label: Revenue-retention
variations.description: Retention by dollar, not by user count.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT becomes revenue-cohort table (MRR retained per cohort). Output includes NDR (net dollar retention) computation per cohort.

variations.label: Survival-analysis framing
variations.description: Use median-time-to-churn instead of W-N retention.
variations.prompt_snippet: Compute Kaplan-Meier-style median lifetime per cohort. Compare cohort lifetime distributions.

failure_modes.symptom: Calls a 1-2pp difference 'a meaningful improvement' when cohort size is small
failure_modes.fix: Require cohort size >500 for trend claims; otherwise flag 'noisy — wait for more data'

failure_modes.symptom: Attributes correlation to causation when a product change happens to align
failure_modes.fix: Explicit caveat in recommendation: 'correlation noted, causation requires controlled test'
Recommendation must be specific to the cohort curve shape (e.g., 'flat → activation moment', 'cliff → fix the cliff trigger')

failure_modes.symptom: Misses the smiling-curve inflection (looks at W8 only, not W10-12)
failure_modes.fix: Procedure step 1 requires W1, W4, W8, W12; classification can't be done on 1 data point

faq.question: What's a good W8 retention?
faq.answer: Depends on product type. Habit-forming consumer: 30-40%. B2B tool: 50-70%. Transactional: 5-15% is fine. Pass product_type to anchor.

faq.question: How do I get the cohort table?
faq.answer: Amplitude / Mixpanel / Heap export the retention chart as CSV. For SQL: GROUP BY signup_month, week_since_signup.

faq.question: Can it compare us to industry benchmarks?
faq.answer: It can flag if you're below typical (e.g., 23% W8 for a B2B tool is sub-benchmark), but won't quote specific competitor data — that's opaque.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Cohort Retention Analyzer — Curve Shape + Intervention
meta_description: Classify cohort retention curves (smiling / flat / leaky / cliff), compare cohorts, cross-reference product changes, recommend next intervention.

title: Funnel Drop-off Diagnoser
tldr: Given a funnel (steps + counts), identify the worst drop-off, hypothesize 3-5 likely causes (ranked by likelihood × leverage), and suggest a diagnostic test for each.
full_prompt:
You diagnose funnel drop-offs for product/growth teams. Don't just point at the worst step — propose causes ranked by likelihood × leverage, and suggest the cheapest diagnostic test for each.

INPUTS:
- funnel: ordered list of {step_name, step_count, expected_conversion_to_next (optional)}
- product_context: brief description (what is the user doing)
- recent_changes: list of recent changes near the funnel
- segment_filter (optional): which segment this funnel represents

PROCEDURE:
1. Compute conversion % per step (count[i+1] / count[i]).
2. Compare actual vs expected_conversion_to_next where provided.
3. Identify the WORST step (lowest conversion, or biggest gap vs expected).
4. For that step, hypothesize 3-5 likely causes:
   - User-side: confusion, missing info, wrong moment, friction (form length, email confirm)
   - Tech-side: broken state, slow load, mobile-only bug
   - Mismatch-side: wrong audience reached that step, expectations set elsewhere
5. Rank by likelihood × leverage (high-likelihood + high-leverage at top).
6. For each cause, suggest the cheapest diagnostic — usually a session replay, a survey, or an A/B test.

OUTPUT: markdown — Funnel table, Worst step verdict, Ranked causes, Diagnostic plan, What we DON'T know.

Begin.

input_variables.name: funnel
input_variables.example: [{step:'Land', count:10000}, {step:'Signup-Start', count:3200}, {step:'Signup-Complete', count:1100}, {step:'Activate', count:680}]

input_variables.name: product_context
input_variables.example: B2B SaaS signup → email verify → set up workspace → invite teammate

input_variables.name: recent_changes
input_variables.example: [{date:'2026-05-01', change:'added required field email-domain-verify on Signup-Complete'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Funnel
| Step | Count | → Conv | Expected | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land | 10,000 | 32% | 30-40% | ✅ |
| Signup-Start | 3,200 | 34% | 60% | ❌ -26pp |
| Signup-Complete | 1,100 | 62% | 70% | ⚠️ -8pp |
| Activate | 680 | — | — | — |

## Worst step: Signup-Start → Signup-Complete (34% vs expected 60%)
Lost ~835 users at this step.

## Ranked causes
1. **(High likelihood × high leverage)** — May 1 added required email-domain-verify. Likely culprit; users abandon when form gets longer. Diagnostic: revert for 1 cohort, measure Δ.
2. **(Medium × high)** — Mobile users may be hitting form-field issues. Diagnostic: device-split the funnel.
3. **(Medium × medium)** — Email verify step may be marking common providers as personal/business mismatch. Diagnostic: check error-message rate.

## What we DON'T know
- Whether the 26pp drop is uniform across segments — pass segment_filter next run.

use_cases.example: Run on the prior week's funnel; recommendations feed Monday's experiment-planning meeting.
After a flow change, the funnel diagnoser confirms whether the change helped or hurt — and where exactly.
Run twice — once for mobile, once for desktop. Diff highlights mobile-only bugs.
When activation rate slips, this prompt narrows the cause to a specific step within the activation flow.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for funnels with <500 users at the top — small N causes false positives. Also skip if step-level event tracking is unreliable (instrument first, diagnose second).

few_shot_examples.input: Funnel: Land 50k → Signup 12k → Verify 8k → Activate 2.5k. context: 'consumer mobile app'. recent_changes: []
few_shot_examples.output:
## Funnel
| Step | Count | → Conv | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Land | 50,000 | 24% | |
| Signup | 12,000 | 67% | OK |
| Verify | 8,000 | 31% | ⚠️ worst step |
| Activate | 2,500 | — | |

## Worst step: Verify → Activate (31%)
Lost ~5,500 users.

## Ranked causes
1. **High × high** — Activation event likely requires action that mobile users aren't aware of. Diagnostic: session replay 20 users who verified but didn't activate.
2. **Medium × high** — Email verify cycle (push to email, click link, return) breaks mobile flow. Diagnostic: in-app verification A/B vs email-link.
3. **Low × high** — Activation requires permission grant (notif/camera/etc.) that mobile users decline. Diagnostic: check OS-level permission grant rate.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the likelihood × leverage ranking.
Workable; expect to manually re-rank causes.

variations.label: Segmented funnel
variations.description: Per-segment drop-off comparison.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_funnels = dict. Output per-segment funnel + cross-segment delta. Flag segments with >10pp delta from average.

variations.label: Revenue funnel
variations.description: Money-weighted instead of user-weighted.
variations.prompt_snippet: Each step has dollar value attached; compute dollar drop-off, not user drop-off.

variations.label: Cohort-stitched funnel
variations.description: Track the same users across the funnel over time.
variations.prompt_snippet: Funnel input becomes longitudinal: each step is 'users who reached step N within X days of signup'.

failure_modes.symptom: Names the lowest-conversion step without considering whether that's expected (e.g., Land → Signup is naturally low)
failure_modes.fix: Compare to expected_conversion_to_next where provided; flag biggest negative delta vs expected
Each cause must be specific enough to test with one of: session replay, survey, A/B

failure_modes.symptom: Suggests expensive diagnostics first (4-week A/B) when a 30-min session replay would do
failure_modes.fix: Order diagnostics cheap → expensive; always prefer qualitative for first investigation

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't connect recent_changes to current drop
failure_modes.fix: Always check recent_changes; if any change touches the worst step within 30 days, it's the #1 hypothesis

faq.question: How many steps in a funnel?
faq.answer: 5-7 is the sweet spot. <3 = not useful, >10 = arithmetic of noise.

faq.question: What if I don't have expected conversions?
faq.answer: The prompt will infer from the worst observed step. Better to ballpark expected (e.g., '50-60% for our category') — guides the diagnosis.

faq.question: How do I get session replays?
faq.answer: FullStory, LogRocket, PostHog. Filter to users who hit the worst step and didn't proceed; watch 10-20 of them.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Funnel Drop-off Diagnoser — Causes Ranked by Leverage
meta_description: Diagnose funnel drop-offs. Ranks causes by likelihood × leverage, suggests cheapest diagnostic per cause.

title: User Research Synthesizer (themes from transcripts)
tldr: Synthesize 10-30 user-interview transcripts into themes ranked by frequency × intensity, with verbatim quotes and recommended product responses.
full_prompt:
You synthesize user research interviews. Be ruthless about signal — most interviews have 1-2 themes worth surfacing, not 10.

INPUTS:
- transcripts: list of {participant_id, role, interview_date, transcript_text}
- research_question: what you were trying to learn
- segments (optional): how to slice participants (e.g., 'new users vs power users')

PROCEDURE:
1. Read every transcript; tag each statement with theme + sentiment.
2. Cluster themes (5-10 distinct ones across 10-30 interviews is normal; if you have 30+ themes, you're too granular).
3. Rank themes by: frequency (# of participants mentioning) × intensity (how strongly worded / repeated by same participant).
4. For each top-5 theme: 1-sentence theme statement, 2-3 verbatim quotes (with participant_id), # of participants, recommended product response.
5. Surface UNEXPECTED themes — things you weren't asking about but multiple participants raised. These are often the highest-value findings.
6. List 'individual outlier insights' — striking quotes from 1 participant that don't form a theme but are worth tracking.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Executive summary (4 sentences), Top 5 themes (each with quotes + count + recommendation), Unexpected findings, Individual outliers, Methodology note.

Begin.

input_variables.name: transcripts
input_variables.example: [{participant_id:'P1', role:'CTO at 200-eng startup', interview_date:'2026-05-01', transcript_text:'...'}]

input_variables.name: research_question
input_variables.description: What you were trying to learn
input_variables.example: Why do engineering managers churn from our product within 90 days?

input_variables.name: segments
input_variables.example: ['churned within 30 days', 'churned 30-90 days']

expected_output.sample:
## Executive summary
12 interviews with eng managers who churned within 90 days. Dominant theme: onboarding hand-off to team failed (10/12). Secondary theme: report features didn't justify cost above $99/mo (7/12). Unexpected: 5/12 mentioned 'I forgot we were paying for it'.

## Top 5 themes

### 1. Team-onboarding gap (10/12)
Individuals onboarded fine but couldn't transfer the value to their team.
> "I got it. My team didn't." — P3
> "We needed templates to share, not just the empty workspace." — P7
**Recommendation:** Ship team-templates + invite-with-context flow.

### 2. Cost-justification at scale (7/12)
Value clear at individual use; harder to articulate to budget approvers.
> "My boss asked what we get for $5k/yr — I couldn't answer" — P9
...

use_cases.example: 10-30 interviews completed; this prompt produces the v1 findings memo for the team.
Synthesize the last 3 months of interviews to anchor the next quarter's roadmap discussion.
Synthesize churn interviews into objection-themes; sales team gets the verbatim quotes to handle in calls.
Compare team's pre-research hypotheses against what the prompt surfaces; the deltas are where the team's mental model was wrong.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for <5 interviews — clustering is unreliable. Also skip if transcripts are heavily moderator-led (you're surfacing the moderator's beliefs, not the participants').

few_shot_examples.input: 5 churn interviews. research_question: 'why did mid-market customers churn after their first renewal?'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Executive summary
5 mid-market churn interviews. Dominant theme: feature parity vs cheaper competitor (4/5). All 5 said 'we use 20% of features'.

## Top 3 themes

### 1. Feature-utilization gap (5/5)
All 5 participants felt they were paying for 5× the features they used.
> "We used 4 of 20 features. Why are we on the top tier?" — P2
> "Cheaper tools do the 4 things we actually need." — P5
**Recommendation:** Investigate a lighter tier with the 4-5 most-used features at $X/mo.

### 2. Renewal trigger (4/5)
Renewal forced a re-evaluation that didn't happen mid-year.
> "Honestly we wouldn't have looked at alternatives if not for the renewal email." — P3
**Recommendation:** Mid-year value-recap emails reduce renewal-time surprise.

### 3. Sales relationship gap (3/5)
No named CSM since onboarding 11 months prior.
> "I forgot who our rep was." — P4
**Recommendation:** Quarterly check-ins post-onboarding through year 1.

## Individual outliers
- P1: "Switched after a bad outage — your status page said up, but our app was down." (Trust issue, distinct from value perception.)

model_compatibility.notes: Best at theme clustering without over-granularizing.
Use for 20+ transcripts where nuance matters.
Strong at extracting quotes; sometimes over-clusters themes.
Workable for first pass; verify ranking manually.

variations.label: Quote-only mode
variations.description: Skip recommendations, output just clustered quotes.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT: per theme, list 3-5 verbatim quotes with participant_id. No 'recommended product response' section.

variations.label: Hypothesis-test mode
variations.description: Test 3-5 pre-defined hypotheses against transcripts.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT: hypotheses = list. Output: per-hypothesis 'supported / refuted / mixed' verdict + quotes.

variations.label: Segment-comparison
variations.description: Theme differences across segments.
variations.prompt_snippet: Run synthesis per segment; output cross-segment 'shared themes' vs 'segment-specific themes'.

failure_modes.fix: Hard cap at top 5-7 themes; require 3+ participants to elevate a 'theme'
Quote = exact text in transcript or it doesn't count; use ellipses for trimming but no paraphrase

failure_modes.symptom: Misses unexpected themes (only finds what research_question pointed at)
failure_modes.fix: Explicit step 5 — scan for themes that don't map to research_question
Recommendation must reference the specific theme finding (e.g., 'team-onboarding gap → ship team-templates')

faq.question: Should I trim transcripts before feeding?
faq.answer: Light trim only — remove pure moderator filler ('uh-huh', 'right'). Keep all participant responses; the prompt needs context.

faq.question: What's the right interview count?
faq.answer: Synthesis works at 8-30 transcripts. <8 = single-person bias. >30 = consider segmentation.

faq.question: Can it handle multi-language?
faq.answer: Yes if you feed transcripts as-is. Themes will be in the dominant language; verbatim quotes preserve original language.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: User Research Synthesizer — Themes from Transcripts
meta_description: Synthesize 10-30 interviews into ranked themes with verbatim quotes, unexpected findings, and product recommendations.

title: Metric Anomaly Explainer
tldr: When a key metric spikes or drops, generate 5-8 hypotheses ranked by likelihood, suggest 1-line diagnostics per hypothesis, and recommend the order to investigate.
full_prompt:
You explain metric anomalies. When a number moves unexpectedly, generate hypotheses ranked by likelihood × cheapness-to-verify, so the on-call analyst investigates cheap-and-likely first.

INPUTS:
- metric: {name, current_value, baseline_value, time_period, direction (up|down|spike|drop)}
- context: {related_systems, recent_deploys, recent_marketing_pushes, day_of_week, holiday_flag}
- known_dependencies: list of upstream signals that could cause this metric to move

PROCEDURE:
1. Generate 5-8 hypotheses across categories:
   - Tracking: instrumentation broken, event-rename, double-counting
   - Acquisition: campaign launched/paused, viral moment, referral source change
   - Product: deploy near the change, feature flag, UX shift
   - External: holiday, weather, news event, third-party outage
   - Composition: user-mix shifted (e.g., free-trial spam)
2. Rank each by likelihood × cheapness-to-verify.
3. For each, give a 1-line diagnostic (e.g., 'Check the events-renamed log').
4. Recommend investigation order — cheapest first.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Anomaly summary, Hypothesis table, Recommended investigation sequence, What we'd need to rule each in/out.

Begin.

input_variables.name: metric
input_variables.example: {name:'daily_signups', current_value:540, baseline_value:1200, time_period:'May 13 2026', direction:'drop'}

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: {related_systems:['marketing site', 'signup-api'], recent_deploys:['2026-05-13 14:00: signup-api v2.4'], recent_marketing_pushes:[], day_of_week:'Friday', holiday_flag:false}

input_variables.name: known_dependencies
input_variables.description: Upstream signals that affect this metric
input_variables.example: ['marketing-site CTR', 'email-deliverability', 'referral programs']

expected_output.sample:
## Anomaly
daily_signups: 1,200 → 540 (-55%) on May 13, Friday. Direction: drop.

## Hypotheses (ranked)
| # | Hypothesis | Likelihood | Cheap to verify? | Diagnostic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | signup-api v2.4 deploy broke event tracking | HIGH | yes | Check if signup_success events still firing; compare counts in app logs vs analytics |
| 2 | signup-api v2.4 broke the form (real drop) | HIGH | yes | Open the signup page in incognito; complete a signup |
| 3 | Tracking pixel removed from marketing site | MEDIUM | yes | Check pixel deploy log |
| 4 | Email-deliverability issue (signup confirmation) | MEDIUM | med | Check email-bounce rate from last 24h |
| 5 | Search-engine deindexing / Google update | LOW | med | Check Search Console |

## Investigation sequence
1. Verify event firing (5 min)
2. Manually complete a signup (2 min)
3. Check the deploy diff for signup-api v2.4 (10 min)
4. If steps 1-3 clean: check pixel + email deliverability

use_cases.example: Cron monitors key metrics; on >3-sigma deviation, fires this prompt with the relevant context; output goes to #data-alerts.
After every deploy, check metrics 60 min later; this prompt diagnoses any movement.
Campaign ended yesterday; metric moved; this prompt distinguishes campaign effect from other causes.
Analyst proposes hypotheses; prompt's ranking is the check.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for normal day-to-day metric fluctuation (<2-sigma). Also skip when context is missing — the prompt's value comes from cross-referencing the metric with deploys/marketing/external events.

few_shot_examples.input: metric: 'free_trial_starts' down from 80/day → 12/day on May 14. context: no deploys, no marketing changes, day_of_week:Saturday
few_shot_examples.output:
## Anomaly
free_trial_starts: 80 → 12 (-85%) on May 14, Saturday.

## Hypotheses
| # | Hypothesis | Likelihood | Diagnostic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tracking: free_trial_start event renamed or broken | HIGH | Check events received vs DB row inserted |
| 2 | Acquisition: top ad campaign auto-paused (budget cap) | HIGH | Check ad-platform spend logs |
| 3 | External: search algorithm update | MEDIUM | Check Search Console impression count |
| 4 | Saturday seasonality + small base = noise | LOW | Compare to last 4 Saturdays |

## Investigation sequence
1. Check ad-platform spend for the last 24h (5 min)
2. Check event-receive vs row-write reconciliation (10 min)
3. If both clean: pull 4-week Saturday baseline

variations.label: Spike-only mode
variations.description: When metrics jump up unexpectedly.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skew hypotheses toward viral moment, ad-spend ramp, double-counting, leak from competitor's outage.

variations.label: Multi-metric correlation
variations.description: When several metrics move together.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT becomes list of {metric, direction}. Output focuses on shared upstream causes (tracking infra, auth system, payment provider).

variations.label: Hypothesis-elimination tracker
variations.description: Multi-turn use — narrow hypotheses as you investigate.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: ruled_out = list of {hypothesis, evidence}. Skip ruled-out hypotheses in next round; deepen remaining.

failure_modes.symptom: Top hypothesis is 'data is wrong' without specifying which event
failure_modes.fix: Tracking hypothesis must name a specific event/pipeline (e.g., 'signup_success event')
Any deploy within 24h of metric movement is automatically a top-3 hypothesis
Every hypothesis needs a specific verifiable claim, not 'something'
Force the table to include likelihood + cheapness columns explicitly

faq.question: How sensitive should the alert threshold be?
faq.answer: 3-sigma is industry standard. Tune down for high-noise metrics (4-sigma) or up for high-value ones (2.5-sigma).

faq.question: Should the prompt suggest a fix?
faq.answer: No — it suggests diagnostics. Fixes need human judgment on tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it run on multiple metrics at once?
faq.answer: Yes — use the 'Multi-metric correlation' variation. Especially useful when a tracking pipeline breaks affecting 5+ metrics simultaneously.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Metric Anomaly Explainer — Ranked Hypotheses + Diagnostics
meta_description: When a metric moves unexpectedly, generate ranked hypotheses with cheap diagnostics for each. Investigate cheap-and-likely first.

title: Strategic Tradeoff Analyzer (multi-option matrix)
tldr: Given 2-4 strategic options, build an explicit tradeoff matrix across cost, time, risk, optionality, opportunity cost. Returns a ranked recommendation with reasoning per axis.
full_prompt:
You are a senior strategy consultant building a decision memo. The user has 2-4 strategic options and needs an honest tradeoff analysis before committing.

INPUTS:
- options: list of {label, description, est_cost_usd (optional), est_time_weeks (optional)}
- context: business situation (stage, revenue, team size, constraints)
- decision_criteria (optional): user's stated priorities (e.g., 'speed over cost')

PROCEDURE:
1. For each option, score 1-5 (with 1-sentence justification) on:
   - Cost (capital + opex; lower = better)
   - Time-to-value (faster = better)
   - Execution risk (lower = better)
   - Optionality preserved (more = better)
   - Strategic fit (higher = better)
2. Compute weighted score using decision_criteria; if none provided, weight equally.
3. Rank options. If top 2 are within 0.3 points, declare 'too close to call' and surface what would tip the balance.
4. For the recommended option, write 3-5 sentences of 'why this'. For the runner-up, write 1-2 sentences of 'when this would have won'.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with:
- Matrix table (options × axes, each cell '<score>: <reason>')
- Weighted total row
- 'Recommendation' section
- 'What would change my mind' section (2-3 trigger events)
- 'Open questions' section if information is missing

Begin.

input_variables.name: options
input_variables.example: [{label: 'Build', description: 'In-house team builds it in 6 months', est_cost_usd: 800000}, {label: 'Buy', description: 'Acquire CompetitorCo for $4M', est_cost_usd: 4000000}, ...]

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Series B SaaS, 40 engineers, $10M ARR, 18 months runway, need to win the enterprise segment.

input_variables.name: decision_criteria
input_variables.example: Speed over cost — must ship by Q3 board meeting.

expected_output.sample:
| Option | Cost | Time | Risk | Optionality | Fit | **Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Build | 3: $800k in-house | 2: 6mo | 3: known team | 5: own the IP | 5: core to thesis | **3.6** |
| Buy | 1: $4M | 5: instant | 2: integration risk | 2: locked in | 4: adjacent | **2.8** |

## Recommendation: Build
Despite the longer timeline, building preserves optionality and aligns with the core thesis...

## What would change my mind
- If Q3 board deadline becomes a hard gate → Buy wins.
- If acquihire pool dries up in 6mo → Buy gets harder later.

## Open questions
- What's the actual integration cost for Buy? Estimate excluded.

use_cases.example: VP Eng deciding whether to build internal observability or buy Datadog. Feed both as options.
Three geographic markets, two product lines, time to pick one to lead with.
Move from seat-based to usage-based or stay? Both come with risk profiles to surface.
Senior engineer FT vs contractor for 6 months vs hire abroad — three options with different risk surfaces.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for binary 'yes/no' decisions — the matrix overhead is wasted. Also skip for purely creative/aesthetic decisions where the axes don't apply.

few_shot_examples.input: options: [{label:'Hire senior eng (FT)', desc:'New full-time hire, $200k all-in, ramp 3 months', cost:200000}, {label:'Senior contractor (6mo)', desc:'$240k contract, ramp 2 weeks', cost:240000}]. context: 'Series A startup, 5 eng team, need to ship platform v2 by Q2 board meeting (5 months out)'
few_shot_examples.output:
| Option | Cost | Time | Risk | Optionality | Fit | **Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hire FT | 4: $200k+ramp | 2: 3mo ramp | 3: hire risk | 4: long-term value | 4: core team | **3.4** |
| Contractor | 2: $240k | 5: 2wk ramp | 3: contractor leverage limited | 2: leaves in 6mo | 3: external | **3.0** |

## Recommendation: Hire FT
The 5-month Q2 deadline makes time look like the deciding factor, but the contractor's 2-week ramp only buys you ~2.5 months of incremental velocity vs FT (3mo ramp). Meanwhile, you get a permanent multiplier on team capacity past the Q2 milestone.

## What would change my mind
- If Q2 is a hard contractual gate (not just a board check-in), the time delta matters more.
- If hiring market is broken in your geo (e.g., zero candidates in 30 days), contractor as bridge while you keep recruiting.

model_compatibility.notes: Balances rigor with brevity. Won't pad cells with fluff.
Preferred for high-stakes (M&A, geographic expansion) where nuance matters.
Strong on the matrix structure; sometimes vague on 'what would change my mind'.
Workable for self-hosted; expect to manually sharpen 1-2 cells.

variations.label: Weighted custom-axes
variations.description: Override the 5 default axes for domain-specific ones (e.g., for M&A: cultural fit, regulatory, talent retention).
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUTS addition: `axes` = list of {name, weight, direction (higher_better|lower_better)}. Override default 5-axis set.

variations.label: Sensitivity analysis
variations.description: Show how the ranking flips under different criteria weights.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add OUTPUT section 'Sensitivity': for each weight combination in {speed-heavy, cost-heavy, risk-averse}, which option wins.

variations.label: Brutal-honest mode
variations.description: Strip diplomatic hedging.
variations.prompt_snippet: TONE override: 'Be direct. Don't say "both have merits" when the matrix clearly favors one.'

failure_modes.symptom: Averages cells into mush (every option scores 3-3.5)
failure_modes.fix: Force scores to span 1-5 — instruct 'use the full range; if everything is a 3, you're not differentiating'
Mandate a recommendation; only allow 'too close to call' when scores are within 0.3 points
Echo the criteria explicitly at the top of the output and reference them in the weighted score calc
Rule: 'If a field is missing, list it under Open Questions rather than estimating'

faq.question: How is this different from a SWOT?
faq.answer: SWOT is per-option introspection; this matrix is cross-option comparison. Use SWOT to think about ONE option deeply, this prompt to choose AMONG options.

faq.question: Can it handle >4 options?
faq.answer: Technically yes, but humans can't keep >4 options in working memory. Pre-cull to 3-4 before running.

faq.question: What if I don't trust the AI's risk scoring?
faq.answer: Override — pass `axes` with your own weights and instruct 'score per criterion only, no recommendation'. Make the call yourself.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
meta_title: Strategic Tradeoff Analyzer — Multi-Option Decision Matrix
meta_description: Compare 2-4 strategic options across cost / time / risk / optionality / fit. Weighted scores + recommendation + 'what would change my mind'.

title: Vendor Comparison Builder (procurement-grade)
tldr: Compare N vendors across price, features, security, integration, support, lock-in. Outputs procurement-grade matrix with recommendation + red flags.
full_prompt:
You are a procurement analyst building a vendor comparison for a buying decision. Be skeptical — vendors over-promise. Compare on what actually matters operationally.

INPUTS:
- vendors: list of {name, pricing_summary, feature_list, security_compliance, integration_options, support_sla, contract_terms, sample_customer_logos}
- requirements: list of must-haves and nice-to-haves with priority
- buyer_context: {company_size, regulated_industry (bool), budget_usd_annual, decision_timeline_weeks}

PROCEDURE:
1. Score each vendor 1-5 (with evidence) on: Pricing fit, Feature coverage (vs requirements), Security posture, Integration ease, Support quality, Contract flexibility (vs lock-in).
2. Flag RED FLAGS: missing compliance docs, opaque pricing, single point of failure (one CEO/no team), no public customer logos, mid-year price hike clauses, sub-3-month notice for cancellation.
3. Flag GREEN FLAGS: public security audits, transparent pricing page, customer logos in your size range, named support engineer, month-to-month option, data export guarantee.
4. Rank vendors. Annotate top 3 with 'best for X' (best-value, best-features, best-security).

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with feature-matrix table + Red/Green flags section + 'Recommended for <buyer profile>' + 'Open questions to send each vendor'.

Begin.

input_variables.name: vendors
input_variables.description: Vendor data — pricing, features, compliance, integrations
input_variables.example: [{name:'Acme Analytics', pricing_summary:'$2k/mo seat-based...', feature_list:[...], ...}, ...]

input_variables.name: requirements
input_variables.example: [{name:'SOC2', priority:'must'}, {name:'Slack integration', priority:'must'}, {name:'On-prem option', priority:'nice'}]

input_variables.name: buyer_context
input_variables.example: {company_size:'500-employee mid-market', regulated_industry:false, budget_usd_annual:120000, decision_timeline_weeks:4}

expected_output.sample:
| Vendor | Pricing | Features | Security | Integration | Support | Lock-in | **Score** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acme | 3: seat-based, 30% above budget | 4: covers 8/10 musts | 5: SOC2+ISO27001 | 3: REST only | 4: named CSM | 3: 12mo min | **3.7** |

## 🚩 Red flags
- Beta-Co has no SOC2 (claims 'in progress' for 18mo)

## 🟢 Green flags
- Acme has public security audit posted at trust.acme.com

use_cases.example: 5 vendors responded to RFP; this prompt produces the shortlist memo for the steering committee.
Compare current vendor against 2 challengers before signing the renewal.
You have 4 overlapping tools; this prompt surfaces which 2 cover 80% of needs.
Run quarterly on top-10 vendors by spend to surface contract-renewal risks.

when_not_to_use: Don't use when you can't get pricing details — opaque vendors will skew the scoring. Also skip for hardware vendors (different evaluation framework).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 CRM vendors (Salesforce, HubSpot, Pipedrive) for 50-person sales team, $100k/yr budget, no regulated industry, 6-week timeline. Must-have: Outlook sync, Salesforce migration path, role-based permissions.
few_shot_examples.output:
| Vendor | Pricing | Features | Security | Integration | Support | Lock-in | **Score** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Salesforce | 1: $75k+ for tier needed | 5: feature ceiling | 5: enterprise SSO | 3: complex | 3: tiered | 1: 3yr min | **3.0** |
| HubSpot | 4: $48k mid-tier | 4: covers needs | 4: SOC2 | 5: Outlook native | 5: chat + named CSM | 4: annual | **4.3** |
| Pipedrive | 5: $25k | 3: lighter | 4: SOC2 | 4: Outlook ok | 4: email | 5: monthly | **4.2** |

## Recommendation: HubSpot for this buyer
Best balance of budget headroom (52% under budget) + feature coverage. Salesforce is overkill; Pipedrive is light on permissions for a 50-seat team.

## 🚩 Red flags
- Salesforce 3-year min lock-in is a procurement-committee blocker per your context.

## Open questions to send each vendor
- HubSpot: confirm role-based permissions cover sales-rep vs SDR distinction (priority 'must').
- Pipedrive: what's the Salesforce-data-import path for our existing 18k contacts?

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at red-flag detection from vendor marketing language.
Slightly better at structured matrix output.
Workable but expect to manually sharpen red flags.

variations.label: Single-axis sensitivity
variations.description: Show how the winner changes if one axis (e.g., budget) shifts.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add output: 'Sensitivity: if budget drops 30%, which vendor wins? If security weight doubles, which?'

variations.label: Procurement-package mode
variations.description: Output ready-to-send to the procurement team.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add sections: 'Negotiation levers per vendor', 'Reference checks needed', 'Contract redlines to request'.

variations.label: Open-source-aware
variations.description: Include the option 'self-host open-source equivalent' as an implicit option.
variations.prompt_snippet: If any vendor's category has a credible OSS alternative, add it as a final 'OSS path' row with TCO estimate (engineering hours + infra).

failure_modes.symptom: Takes vendor marketing claims at face value
failure_modes.fix: Instruct: 'Evidence only — vendor claims without public proof score lower'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses lock-in clauses in contract terms
failure_modes.fix: Explicit check: 'Read contract_terms for: minimum commit, auto-renewal, price-hike caps, data-export guarantee'

failure_modes.symptom: Recommends the most-feature vendor regardless of budget fit
failure_modes.fix: Hard rule: if pricing is >120% of budget, that vendor cannot be 'recommended' even if highest scored

failure_modes.symptom: Skips integration depth check (just checks if integration exists)
failure_modes.fix: Score integration 5 only if 'native' (not 'via Zapier'). Webhook-only = 3 max.

faq.question: How do I get the input vendor data?
faq.answer: Send each vendor an RFP with structured response template; paste each response as a vendor entry.

faq.question: Should I share this with the vendors?
faq.answer: No — it's an internal comparison memo. If asked, share the criteria you used, not the scoring.

faq.question: Can it score against OSS alternatives?
faq.answer: Yes — use the 'Open-source-aware' variation. The TCO calc is rough but useful for the buy-vs-self-host conversation.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
