title: Meeting Decision Recorder (decisions ≠ actions ≠ discussion)
tldr: From meeting notes, separate DECISIONS (with rationale + reversal cost) from action items and discussion. Maintains a permanent decision log for the team.
full_prompt:
You extract DECISIONS from messy meeting notes. Decisions ≠ action items ≠ discussion. Be strict about the difference.

DEFINITIONS:
- DECISION: a binding choice between alternatives. Has an owner, a date, a reversal cost (cheap/medium/expensive).
- ACTION ITEM: a task someone will do. Not a decision unless the act of doing it constitutes a choice.
- DISCUSSION: explored but unresolved. Not a decision; surface to the next agenda.

INPUTS:
- meeting_notes: raw notes (transcript, Zoom AI summary, hand-written)
- meeting_metadata: {date, attendees, meeting_title}
- prior_decisions (optional): list of recent decisions for context (avoids re-recording the same)

PROCEDURE:
1. Identify every candidate decision. For each, check: was a choice made between named alternatives, with an owner?
2. For each confirmed decision, record: title (1 line), context (1-2 sentences), decision (1 sentence), alternatives considered, rationale (1-2 sentences), owner, reversal cost (cheap/medium/expensive).
3. Separately list action items: {who, what, when}.
4. Separately list discussion items that need follow-up: {topic, why unresolved, next agenda}.
5. Cross-reference prior_decisions: flag any new decision that contradicts a prior one.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown sections): 'Decisions made' (numbered, full structure), 'Action items' (table: who/what/when), 'Open for next meeting' (bulleted), 'Contradictions with prior decisions' (only if any).

Begin.

input_variables.name: meeting_notes
input_variables.description: Raw meeting notes — transcript, AI summary, or written notes
input_variables.example: Discussed pricing changes. Sarah proposed moving to usage-based. After 20min debate, agreed to stay seat-based for 2025, revisit Q1 2026. Action: Sarah to draft 2026 pricing memo by Feb 1...

input_variables.name: meeting_metadata
input_variables.example: {date:'2026-05-14', attendees:['Sarah','Tom','Maya'], meeting_title:'Q2 pricing review'}

input_variables.name: prior_decisions
input_variables.example: [{title:'Pricing model 2025', decision:'Stay seat-based through 2025', date:'2025-12-01'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Decisions made

### 1. Pricing model for 2026
- **Context:** Sales pushing usage-based, Eng concerned about complexity.
- **Decision:** Stay seat-based through Q1 2026; revisit Q2 with usage data.
- **Alternatives considered:** Move to usage-based now; hybrid model.
- **Rationale:** Insufficient usage data to model revenue impact of switch.
- **Owner:** Sarah
- **Reversal cost:** medium (annual contracts will renew on current model)

## Action items
| Who | What | When |
|---|---|---|
| Sarah | Draft 2026 pricing memo with usage-data analysis | Feb 1 |

## Open for next meeting
- Hybrid model: nobody had a clear preference. Needs Maya's customer-conversation summary first.

use_cases.example: Run on the meeting's AI-transcript output; the decision log becomes the team's source of truth.
After a long Slack thread, paste the thread + ask: 'Did we actually decide anything?'
Give them the last 6 months of decisions to ramp on team context faster than reading every meeting note.
Run monthly with prior_decisions populated; flag where the team is unintentionally reversing itself.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for brainstorming sessions (no decisions expected). Also skip if the notes are too sparse — 'discussed pricing, agreed to revisit' isn't enough to extract a decision.

few_shot_examples.input: Notes: 'Talked about hiring. Need senior eng + designer. Sarah will post the eng role this week. Designer — Tom thinks contract first, Maya wants FT. Punted to next week. Also: agreed we will NOT hire a PM in Q2 even if revenue grows — Sarah locked this.'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Decisions made

### 1. No PM hire in Q2 regardless of revenue
- **Context:** Recurring debate; revenue growth often triggers premature PM hiring.
- **Decision:** No PM hire in Q2.
- **Alternatives considered:** Hire if revenue >$X (specific target not set in meeting).
- **Rationale:** [missing — note in next meeting]
- **Owner:** Sarah
- **Reversal cost:** cheap (decision can be revisited any time)

## Action items
| Who | What | When |
|---|---|---|
| Sarah | Post senior eng role | This week |

## Open for next meeting
- Designer hiring model: contract-first (Tom) vs FT (Maya). Both need to bring 1 example from similar-stage company.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the decision vs action vs discussion distinction; rarely conflates.
Cheap enough to run on every meeting; occasional miss on subtle decisions.
Tends to over-classify action items as decisions. Tighten with: 'an action item is NOT a decision unless...'.
Workable; expect manual review pass on important meetings.

variations.label: Decision log append-only
variations.description: Output JSON to append to a permanent decision log (Notion DB, etc.).
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT format: JSON array of decisions only. Skip actions/discussion sections.

variations.label: Reversal-cost focus
variations.description: Highlight 'expensive' reversal decisions explicitly.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add at top of output: '⚠️ Hard-to-reverse decisions in this meeting: <count>'. List them first.

variations.label: Stakeholder notification draft
variations.description: Generate stakeholder emails for each decision.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add 'Stakeholder comms' section: 1-paragraph email per decision, addressed to non-attendees who need to know.

failure_modes.fix: Reinforce definition; reject any item missing 'choice between alternatives'

failure_modes.symptom: Records 'we discussed X' as a decision
failure_modes.fix: Discussion without a binding choice goes to 'Open for next meeting'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses decisions buried in long transcripts
failure_modes.fix: Pre-summarize transcripts >5k tokens by extracting candidate-decision sentences first
Add an explicit check step before output: 'Compare every new decision against prior_decisions list'

faq.question: What's a 'reversal cost'?
faq.answer: Cheap = can flip in a week with no public impact. Medium = month-scale, some stakeholder comms. Expensive = quarter-scale, signed contracts, or public commitments.

faq.question: Should every meeting produce decisions?
faq.answer: No — many meetings are status updates or brainstorms. The prompt will return an empty Decisions section, which is honest.

faq.question: How do I use this with Zoom's AI summary?
faq.answer: Paste the AI summary as meeting_notes. Caveat: AI summaries miss subtle decisions; if attendee count is high, also paste the chat transcript.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
meta_title: Meeting Decision Recorder — Extract Decisions from Notes
meta_description: Separate decisions from action items and discussion. Maintains a permanent decision log with rationale + reversal cost per decision.

title: Monthly Investor Update Drafter (honest + scannable)
tldr: Draft a monthly investor update: KPIs with deltas, wins, challenges, asks. ~400 words, scannable, honest about misses without spinning.
full_prompt:
You draft monthly investor updates for early-stage founders. Tone: confident, honest, scannable. No corporate-speak. No spin on misses.

INPUTS:
- month_label: 'May 2026'
- kpis: list of {name, current_value, prior_value, unit, target (optional)}
- wins: 2-5 things that shipped or moved
- challenges: 1-3 honest misses or risks
- asks: 1-3 specific requests from investors (intros, hires, expertise)
- context (optional): stage, runway, prior-month update for continuity

STRUCTURE:
1. **TL;DR** — 2 sentences. What moved this month + one ask.
2. **Numbers** — KPI table with delta vs prior month, color-coded (📈 / 📉 / ➡️).
3. **Wins** — bullets, concrete (no 'made progress on X').
4. **Challenges** — bullets, name the thing honestly. Add 1-line 'what we're doing about it' each.
5. **Asks** — bullets, specific. 'Intro to <name>' not 'help with sales'.
6. **Runway / cash** — 1-line if disclosed in inputs.

RULES:
- 350-450 words total. Investors skim.
- Use 'we' not 'I' even if it's a solo founder.
- Never use: 'rest assured', 'going forward', 'leveraged', 'doubled down', 'pivoted'.
- Misses go in Challenges, never reframed as Wins.
- Asks should be answerable in 30 seconds by the investor.

Begin.

input_variables.name: month_label
input_variables.example: May 2026

input_variables.name: kpis
input_variables.example: [{name:'MRR', current:42000, prior:38500, unit:'USD'}, {name:'Active accounts', current:127, prior:115, unit:'count'}]

input_variables.name: wins
input_variables.example: ['Shipped self-serve onboarding', 'Closed Acme Corp ($24k ACV)', 'Hired senior eng (starts June 1)']

input_variables.name: challenges
input_variables.example: ['Churn ticked up to 3.2% from 2.1%', 'Q2 hiring slower than plan — 1 of 3 roles filled']

input_variables.name: asks
input_variables.example: ['Intro to Head of RevOps at <company>', 'Intro to a designer agency that has worked with B2B SaaS at $50k-200k ACV']

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Series A, 14mo runway, raising in 6-8mo

expected_output.sample:
## OSS AI Hub — May 2026 update

**TL;DR:** MRR grew 9% MoM to $42k; closed Acme as our first $24k ACV deal. Asking for intros to design agencies (see below).

## Numbers
| Metric | May | Apr | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| MRR | $42,000 | $38,500 | 📈 +9% |
| Active accounts | 127 | 115 | 📈 +10% |
| Churn | 3.2% | 2.1% | 📉 +1.1pp |

## Wins
- Shipped self-serve onboarding — first 12 self-serve signups closed without sales.
- Closed Acme Corp ($24k ACV).
- Hired senior eng, starts June 1.

## Challenges
- Churn ticked up to 3.2% from 2.1%. Root cause: 2 customers downsized after acquihires. Not a product issue, but we're adding pre-acquihire signal-tracking.
- Q2 hiring slower than plan: 1 of 3 roles filled. Designer search is the bottleneck.

## Asks
- Intro to a design agency with B2B SaaS experience at $50k–200k ACV range. We've talked to 3, all priced at enterprise.
- Intro to Head of RevOps at <name>.

Runway: 14 months. Raising in 6-8 months.

use_cases.example: Founder fills in inputs at month-end; output goes directly to investor mailing list.
Run before quarterly board meeting; the update is the 1-pager attached to the calendar invite.
Same content sent to team as 'where we stand'; investor and team versions diverge only in the Asks section.
Run on the last 12 months of inputs to surface the narrative arc for the deck.

when_not_to_use: Don't use the same prompt for board updates with corporate-governance reporting — those need more structure. Also skip for first-month-of-company updates; there's not enough signal yet.

few_shot_examples.input: month: April 2026, kpis: [{MRR, 38500, 35000, USD}, {accounts, 115, 102, count}, {churn, 2.1%, 1.8%, pct}]. wins: ['Beta launched to 30 customers', 'Series A term sheet signed']. challenges: ['Sales cycle still 6+ weeks for enterprise — too slow']. asks: ['Intro to RevOps-savvy advisor']
few_shot_examples.output:
## OSS AI Hub — April 2026 update

**TL;DR:** Term sheet signed (announcing in May). Beta opened to 30 customers; sales cycle is the constraint to unlock.

## Numbers
| Metric | Apr | Mar | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| MRR | $38,500 | $35,000 | 📈 +10% |
| Active accounts | 115 | 102 | 📈 +13% |
| Churn | 2.1% | 1.8% | ➡️ +0.3pp |

## Wins
- Series A term sheet signed (announcing in May update).
- Beta launched to 30 customers — 24 active in week 1.

## Challenges
- Enterprise sales cycle is 6+ weeks (target: 3). Root cause: procurement + security review. Hiring a RevOps lead to shorten the procurement-side timeline.

## Asks
- Intro to a RevOps-savvy advisor who has shortened enterprise sales cycles at Series A → Series B stage.

model_compatibility.notes: Best balance of concision + honesty. Won't reframe misses.
Slightly more verbose; force the 350-450 word cap.
Cheap monthly run; verify the asks are specific enough.
Workable for self-hosted; tone often skews formal.

variations.label: Pre-raise tightening
variations.description: When raising in <3 months, tone shifts to narrative + traction.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add a 'Why now' section above Numbers — 2-3 sentences on the market moment. Tighten Challenges to '... here's what we're doing' (no dwelling).

variations.label: Down-round / hard month
variations.description: When KPIs slipped, lean into honesty.
variations.prompt_snippet: TL;DR override: lead with the miss. 'Tough month — MRR down 4%; here's what changed and what we're doing.' Investors respect honesty over spin.

variations.label: Email-formatted output
variations.description: Output as a ready-to-send email with subject line.
variations.prompt_snippet: Wrap in: 'Subject: <Company> <Month> update — <one-line headline>'. Add 'Hi <investors>,' opener and signature block.

failure_modes.symptom: Spins misses as wins ('we learned valuable lessons')
failure_modes.fix: Forbid the phrases listed in RULES; reject any 'silver lining' framing of a miss

failure_modes.symptom: Asks are too generic ('help with growth')
failure_modes.fix: Require asks to be answerable in 30 seconds — name a person or company
Strict 350-450 cap; if going over, drop the second-tier win, not the challenge

failure_modes.symptom: KPI deltas wrong direction (e.g., reports churn down when up)
failure_modes.fix: Add explicit check: 'If higher_is_worse for metric, invert delta direction'

faq.question: How honest is too honest?
faq.answer: Investors prefer 'we missed X, here's what we're doing' over 'great month!' Use the prompt's rules — they're calibrated to investor norms.

faq.question: Should I CC the team on this?
faq.answer: Yes, after sending — keeps them aligned on the public story. Don't BCC investors on team updates though.

faq.question: What if my numbers look terrible?
faq.answer: Use the 'Down-round / hard month' variation. Investors are 10× more freaked by a missing update than by an honest tough month.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library

title: RFP Response Builder (capability-mapped)
tldr: Generate a structured RFP response from a question list + your company capability deck. Maps each requirement to a capability with citations and surfaces gaps.
full_prompt:
You build enterprise RFP responses for a B2B SaaS vendor. The buyer asked dozens of questions; you have a capability deck and prior RFP answers. Your job: map each requirement to a capability with citations, surface gaps honestly, and produce responses that pass a procurement review.

INPUTS:
- rfp_questions: structured list — [{section, question_id, question_text, criticality (must|should|nice)}]
- capability_library: list of {capability_name, evidence_links, security_docs, customer_proof}
- prior_rfp_answers (optional): list of {question_pattern, prior_answer} for reuse
- company_context: {industry_specialization, customer_size_focus, competitive_differentiators}

PROCEDURE:
1. For each question, find the best-matching capability. If multiple match, pick the one with strongest evidence.
2. Draft an answer (2-5 sentences) with: (a) direct answer, (b) supporting evidence (cite capability_name + evidence_link), (c) customer proof if available.
3. If no capability matches, mark the question 'GAP' and note: 'partial coverage via <closest>', or 'true gap — recommend roadmap discussion'.
4. For 'must' criticality questions, never bluff — if GAP, be explicit.
5. Reuse prior_rfp_answers verbatim where the question_pattern matches, then adapt to this RFP's tone.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with sections matching RFP sections. Each question: '**Q (criticality):** ...' / '**A:** ...' / '**Evidence:** ...' / Gap badge if applicable.

Append a SUMMARY at top: total questions, # answered fully, # partial gaps, # true gaps, % must-criticality covered.

Begin.

input_variables.name: rfp_questions
input_variables.description: Structured RFP questions with sections and criticality
input_variables.example: [{section:'Security', question_id:'S-12', question_text:'Describe your SOC 2 compliance program', criticality:'must'}]

input_variables.name: capability_library
input_variables.example: [{capability_name:'SOC2 Type II', evidence_links:['trust.example.com/soc2'], security_docs:['SOC2-2026-report.pdf'], customer_proof:'Used by 12 Fortune-500 customers'}]

input_variables.name: prior_rfp_answers
input_variables.example: [{question_pattern:'SOC 2 compliance', prior_answer:'We maintain SOC 2 Type II...'}]

input_variables.name: company_context
input_variables.example: {industry_specialization:'healthcare SaaS', customer_size_focus:'mid-market 200-5000 employees', competitive_differentiators:['HIPAA-native', 'no-PHI-in-logs']}

expected_output.sample:
## SUMMARY
- Total questions: 47
- Answered fully: 41
- Partial gaps: 4
- True gaps: 2
- Must-criticality coverage: 96% (24 of 25)

## Security

**Q (must):** Describe your SOC 2 compliance program.
**A:** We maintain SOC 2 Type II, audited annually by Schellman. Report covers Security, Availability, and Confidentiality TSCs. Current report dated Jan 2026, next audit Jan 2027.
**Evidence:** trust.example.com/soc2 · SOC2-2026-Schellman-report.pdf · Used by 12 Fortune-500 customers including Acme, Beta, Gamma.

**Q (must):** Do you support BYOK (bring your own key) for encryption?
**A:** ⚠️ **PARTIAL GAP** — We support AWS KMS customer-managed keys. True BYOK with HSM is on the H1 2027 roadmap.
**Evidence:** docs.example.com/security/encryption · BYOK roadmap

use_cases.example: Sales team receives 47-question RFP Friday, response due Monday. This prompt produces the v1 in <1 hour for SE review.
Run with last 5 RFPs as input; surface which capabilities show up most often and which evidence links are missing.
Aggregate 'true gap' markers across 20 RFPs to identify the top 5 capabilities to build next.
New AE gets the prior_rfp_answers library; the prompt becomes their cheat sheet.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for RFPs requiring legal-document attachments (MSA, DPA) — those need lawyer review, not prompt-based generation. Also skip for RFPs without structured questions (paragraphs of prose).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 questions on Security section: 'SOC 2?', 'GDPR compliance?', 'Pen-test schedule?'. capability_library has SOC2 + GDPR but no pen-test history.

**Q (must):** SOC 2 compliance?
**A:** SOC 2 Type II, audited annually by Schellman, current report dated Jan 2026.
**Evidence:** trust.example.com/soc2

**Q (must):** GDPR compliance?
**A:** Fully GDPR-compliant. EU data residency available, DPA template at legal.example.com/dpa, Data Protection Officer: dpo@example.com.
**Evidence:** legal.example.com/gdpr · DPA-template-v3.pdf

**Q (must):** Pen-test schedule?
**A:** ⚠️ **GAP** — We do not currently run scheduled third-party pen-tests. Internal security review is annual; external pen-test is on the H2 2026 roadmap. Happy to discuss the timeline.
**Evidence:** N/A — recommend roadmap conversation

model_compatibility.notes: Best at the gap-honesty calibration; won't bluff on must-criticality.
Preferred for $500k+ RFPs where precision matters.
Strong at the structured output; sometimes hedges gaps as 'partial' when they're true gaps.
Workable for self-hosted; expect manual review of every 'gap' label.

variations.label: Compliance-only mode
variations.description: Filter to security/compliance questions only.
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter rfp_questions to section in {security, compliance, privacy}. Skip other sections.

variations.label: Win-themes mode
variations.description: After answers, generate 3 'win themes' to thread through the cover letter.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add a final section: 'Top 3 win themes for cover letter' — derived from capability_library + company_context's competitive_differentiators.

variations.label: Multi-language

failure_modes.symptom: Bluffs on capabilities not in the library
failure_modes.fix: Hard rule: 'If capability_name not in input library, mark GAP. Never invent.'
Every answer must cite at least one evidence_link or customer_proof from input
Criticality must inform the depth — must = 3-5 sentences, nice = 1-2 sentences

failure_modes.symptom: Misses cross-section consistency (says 'no PHI' in security, then 'we log PHI' in audit-trail section)
failure_modes.fix: Run a final consistency pass: 'Check that capability claims don't contradict across sections'

faq.question: How do I build the capability_library?
faq.answer: Start with your top 5 RFPs from last year. Extract every distinct capability mentioned, with its evidence link. Maintain as a Notion database; update quarterly.

faq.question: Should I send the raw prompt output?
faq.answer: No — always SE-review. The prompt's v1 is 90% there; the 10% is product-nuance the prompt can't know.

faq.question: How do I handle questions about future roadmap items?
faq.answer: Be specific. 'On the H2 2026 roadmap' is acceptable; 'we're considering' is not. Procurement reviewers can sniff vagueness.

title: OKR Quarterly Drafter (objectives + measurable key results)
tldr: Convert raw quarterly priorities into well-formed OKRs: 3-5 objectives, each with 2-4 measurable key results, time-bound and grading-ready.
full_prompt:
You convert messy quarterly priorities into well-formed OKRs that pass a 'can I grade this' test.

DEFINITIONS (strict):
- OBJECTIVE: qualitative, ambitious, time-bound, memorable. Inspires.
- KEY RESULT: quantitative, measurable, has a baseline + target, has an owner. Provable.
- ANTI-PATTERN: 'KR' that is actually a task. ('Launch X' is a task, not a KR. 'Achieve N users for X by Q-end' is a KR.)

INPUTS:
- raw_priorities: free-form list of what the team wants to accomplish this quarter
- team_context: {role, team_size, prior_quarter_okr_score, current_metrics_baseline}
- timeframe: {quarter_label, start_date, end_date}

PROCEDURE:
1. Cluster raw_priorities into 3-5 themes. Each theme becomes an Objective.
2. For each Objective, derive 2-4 Key Results from the raw text:
   - Pull a metric (or invent one if implied)
   - Set a baseline (from current_metrics_baseline)
   - Set a stretch target (50% probability of hitting — should not be sandbagged)
   - Assign an owner (from raw text if named; otherwise '<role>')
3. Validate every KR: can it be graded 0-1.0 at Q-end? If no, reframe or drop.
4. Add a 'WHAT DIDN'T MAKE IT' section with priorities that aren't on this quarter's OKRs — and why.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with Objectives numbered, KRs lettered, each KR shown as 'Metric: baseline → target. Owner: X.' Stretch annotation if applicable.

Begin.

input_variables.name: raw_priorities
input_variables.description: Free-form list of what the team wants to accomplish
input_variables.example: Ship search v2, hire 2 mid-level eng, reduce churn, launch enterprise tier, get to $1M ARR, fix the onboarding mess, write a book about scaling...

input_variables.name: team_context
input_variables.description: Role, team size, prior OKR score, current metrics
input_variables.example: {role:'CEO', team_size:18, prior_quarter_okr_score:0.65, current_metrics_baseline:{ARR:850000, churn:3.2, eng_team_size:8}}

input_variables.name: timeframe
input_variables.example: {quarter_label:'Q3 2026', start_date:'2026-07-01', end_date:'2026-09-30'}

expected_output.sample:
# Q3 2026 OKRs

## Objective 1: Cross the $1M ARR line by Q3 close
- **KR 1a:** ARR $850k → $1.05M. Owner: CEO. Stretch: $1.1M.
- **KR 1b:** Churn 3.2% → 2.0%. Owner: Head of CS.
- **KR 1c:** Enterprise pipeline coverage 2× → 3× quota. Owner: Sales.

## Objective 2: Ship search v2 to general availability
- **KR 2a:** Search v2 % of total queries served: 0% → 50%. Owner: Eng lead.
- **KR 2b:** Search p99 latency: 800ms → 200ms. Owner: Eng lead.

## What didn't make it (and why)
- 'Launch enterprise tier': sequenced after we hit $1M ARR (Q4 candidate).
- 'Write a book': not Q-scope; move to personal projects list.

use_cases.example: Founders drop raw priorities into the prompt at end of planning offsite; OKR doc is published by Monday.
Company OKRs go in as input; the prompt drafts team-level OKRs that ladder up. Repeat per team.
Pass last quarter's OKRs + actuals; prompt grades each and proposes next-quarter adjustments.
Existing OKRs feel like a task list; this prompt re-frames the 'tasks-as-KRs' into proper KRs.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for OKRs at the individual-contributor level — better to use personal goal frameworks. Also skip for early-stage startups (<5 people); OKR overhead exceeds the benefit.

few_shot_examples.input: raw_priorities: 'ship search rewrite, hire designer, reduce p99 latency, fix the deploy pain, talk to 50 customers'. context: 'VP Eng, 12 eng team, last quarter OKR score 0.7, current p99: 850ms, deploys take 45min'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Objective 1: Ship search rewrite to GA quality
- **KR 1a:** Search v2 query share 0% → 75% by Q-end. Owner: Search lead.
- **KR 1b:** Search p99 latency 850ms → 250ms. Owner: Search lead.

## Objective 2: Cut engineering friction
- **KR 2a:** Deploy time 45min → 12min. Owner: Platform lead.
- **KR 2b:** Deploy failure rate 8% → 2%. Owner: Platform lead.

## Objective 3: Build customer-discovery muscle
- **KR 3a:** 50 customer conversations completed, summarized in shared doc. Owner: VP Eng.
- **KR 3b:** 3 product-direction decisions traceable to customer-conversation insights. Owner: VP Eng.

## What didn't make it
- 'Hire designer' — not engineering scope; flagged to CEO for company-level OKR.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the task-vs-KR distinction; rarely lets task-shaped KRs through.
Preferred for company-level OKRs at >100-person scale.
Slightly more lenient on task-shaped KRs; reinforce the anti-pattern rule.
Workable for first draft; expect to manually tighten KR ownership.

variations.label: Mid-quarter rewrite
variations.description: When OKRs are clearly off-track at mid-quarter, propose revisions.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add input `current_okr_scores`. Output: per-OKR 'keep / adjust target / drop' recommendation with reasoning.

variations.label: Cascade-aware
variations.description: Ensure team OKRs ladder to company OKRs.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add input `company_okrs`. Every team Objective must reference which company Objective it serves.

variations.label: Stretch-only mode
variations.description: All KRs at 50% probability (ambitious).
variations.prompt_snippet: Hard rule: every KR target must be at 50% confidence, not 80% confidence (sandbagged).

failure_modes.symptom: KRs are tasks in disguise ('Launch X' instead of 'X reaches N users')
failure_modes.fix: Anti-pattern rule in PROCEDURE step 3; every KR must include a measurable metric
Hard cap at 5 objectives; force prioritization with 'what didn't make it' section

failure_modes.symptom: Sandbagged targets (last quarter's actual = next quarter's target)
failure_modes.fix: Stretch annotation forces explicit reflection; prompt asks 'is this a 50% probability target or 80%?'
Every KR requires a named owner or specific role; reject 'team' as too diffuse

faq.question: How is this different from KPIs?
faq.answer: KPIs are ongoing metrics you watch. OKRs are quarterly ambitions with stretch targets. KPIs are speedometer; OKRs are destination.

faq.question: Should every team have OKRs?
faq.answer: No. OKRs work best for teams shipping cross-functional outcomes. Pure infra/maintenance teams often do better with SLAs.

faq.question: What's a good OKR score?
faq.answer: 0.6-0.7 is healthy — means you set ambitious targets and hit most. >0.85 = you sandbagged. <0.4 = either misaligned plan or external shock.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library

meta_description: Convert raw priorities into well-formed OKRs. 3-5 objectives, 2-4 measurable key results each, baseline + target + owner, grading-ready.

title: Competitive Landscape Mapper (positioning + whitespace)
tldr: Map 5-15 competitors on a 2-axis positioning grid, identify whitespace opportunities, surface emerging threats and dying players.
full_prompt:
You are a competitive-intel analyst building a positioning landscape. Be specific about axes — vague axes ('quality', 'innovation') produce useless maps.

INPUTS:
- competitors: list of {name, segment, est_revenue (optional), funding_stage, pricing_model, primary_users, last_major_release_date, momentum_signals}
- our_company: {name, current_position, target_segment}
- axes (optional): user-provided positioning axes. If not provided, you propose 2 axes that meaningfully differentiate.

PROCEDURE:
1. If axes not provided: propose 2 axes that span the landscape (e.g., 'price' × 'flexibility', not 'good' × 'cheap'). Justify the axes.
2. Place each competitor on the 2x2 grid with reasoning (1-2 sentences per placement).
3. Identify the 4 quadrants. Each quadrant: 'crowded' / 'whitespace' / 'dying' / 'emerging'.
4. Flag EMERGING THREATS: any competitor with strong momentum_signals in your target quadrant.
5. Flag DYING PLAYERS: any competitor with no major_release in 12+ months OR stale_pricing OR contracting headcount.
6. Surface WHITESPACE: which quadrant has the fewest players and matches your target_segment?
7. Position us. Are we in a crowded quadrant? Could we move? At what cost?

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with ASCII grid + per-competitor placement + quadrant summary + recommendations.

Begin.

input_variables.name: competitors
input_variables.example: [{name:'Acme', segment:'enterprise', est_revenue:'$30M ARR', funding_stage:'Series C', pricing_model:'seat-based', last_major_release_date:'2026-03-15', momentum_signals:['hired 12 eng in 90 days', 'partnership with AWS announced']}]

input_variables.name: our_company
input_variables.example: {name:'OSS AI Hub', current_position:'mid-market, free + open-source', target_segment:'developer-first, individual + small teams'}

input_variables.name: axes
input_variables.example: ['pricing model: per-seat → usage-based', 'flexibility: prescriptive → configurable']

expected_output.sample:
## Axes
- **X:** pricing model (per-seat → usage-based)
- **Y:** flexibility (prescriptive → configurable)

## Grid
```
Configurable │ [Self-host OSS]      │ [Custom-build platforms]
             │ • Us            • Foo │ • BarPlatform
─────────────┼──────────────────────┼────────────────────────
             │ • Acme (declining)   │ • BigCo (entrenched)
Prescriptive │ [Vertical SaaS]      │ [Enterprise suites]
             │ Per-seat            Usage-based
```

## Quadrant summary
- **Configurable × Per-seat (us + Foo):** ⚠️ Emerging. Foo's GitHub stars grew 4× in 90 days.
- **Configurable × Usage-based (BarPlatform):** Whitespace. Only 1 player.
- **Prescriptive × Per-seat (Acme):** Dying. No release in 14 months.

## Recommendations
- Defend against Foo (configurable per-seat) — they have 30 days of momentum on us.
- Whitespace in Configurable × Usage-based; question whether it matches our developer-first segment.

use_cases.example: Refresh the landscape every January with the prior year's competitor moves; informs the year's positioning shifts.
Before entering a new segment, run the landscape on incumbents. Where's the whitespace?
Investors want to see the landscape. The grid + whitespace claim grounds the 'why us' slide.
Quarterly check-in: who's gaining momentum? Who's dying? Where should we shift?

when_not_to_use: Don't use for highly fragmented markets (50+ competitors) — a 2-axis grid oversimplifies. Use a tier-based segmentation instead. Also skip in monopoly markets where one player dominates.

few_shot_examples.input: competitors: 6 vector DB players: Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, Chroma, pgvector. our_company: OSS AI Hub directory. axes: ['hosted → self-host', 'specialist → general-purpose']
few_shot_examples.output:
## Grid
```
General-purpose │ • pgvector              │ (whitespace)
                │   (Postgres extension)  │
────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────────
Specialist      │ • Qdrant • Weaviate     │ • Pinecone
                │ • Milvus • Chroma       │   (managed)
                │   Self-host             │  Hosted
```

## Quadrant summary
- **Specialist × Self-host (4 players):** Crowded. Qdrant + Weaviate are gaining; Milvus is enterprise-skewed; Chroma is dev-friendly.
- **General-purpose × Self-host (1 player):** Whitespace-ish. pgvector dominates because Postgres is everywhere.
- **Specialist × Hosted (1 player):** Pinecone owns this corner.
- **General-purpose × Hosted:** True whitespace. Any cloud provider could fill this with a managed Postgres + pgvector tier.

## Emerging threats
- Qdrant momentum (5x GitHub stars in 12mo).

## Recommendations
- We're a directory, not a vector DB — our 'positioning' here is informational. Useful to surface this grid in our `/vector-databases` category page so users can self-navigate.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at proposing specific axes (avoids 'good vs bad' framing).
Preferred for >10 competitors or when the market is technical/nuanced.
Strong matrix output; sometimes vague on momentum signals.
Workable; manual review of placements expected.

variations.label: Threat-only mode
variations.description: Skip placement; just surface threats.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip steps 2-3. Run only step 4 (emerging threats) + step 5 (dying players). Output prioritized threat list.

variations.label: Whitespace-search mode
variations.description: Optimize for finding gaps.
variations.prompt_snippet: Run with 3 different axis pairs (cost×flex, segment×depth, OSS×managed). Surface the whitespace that appears in 2+ of the maps.

variations.label: Investor-deck format
variations.description: Output ready for slide.
variations.prompt_snippet: Output: 1 slide title ('The landscape, May 2026'), 1 grid, 3-bullet 'why we win in this corner', no other prose.

failure_modes.fix: Reject axes that aren't measurable; require axes to span a real spectrum like 'pricing model' or 'deployment model'

failure_modes.symptom: Places all competitors in same quadrant
failure_modes.fix: Force the axes to differentiate; if grid is unbalanced, propose alternative axes

failure_modes.symptom: Misses emerging threats from absence of momentum_signals input
failure_modes.fix: If momentum_signals not provided for a competitor, surface 'data gap — recommend manual check'

failure_modes.symptom: Recommends moving us to a crowded quadrant
failure_modes.fix: Recommendation must factor in cost of repositioning; default is defend current position unless whitespace × match-segment

faq.question: How many competitors should I include?
faq.answer: 5-12 is the sweet spot. <5 = too sparse for a 2x2; >15 = the grid becomes unreadable. Filter to credible threats only.

faq.question: How do I source momentum_signals?
faq.answer: Track 3 signals quarterly: hiring (LinkedIn), product releases (changelogs/blog), public mentions (G2/HN). 10 minutes per competitor.

faq.question: Should I share this with the team?
faq.answer: Yes, but quarterly — not weekly. Competitive obsession kills focus.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library

title: Pricing Page Rewriter (conversion-focused)
tldr: Rewrite a SaaS pricing page for clarity and conversion: tier labels, anchor pricing, feature differentiation, and a 'right one for you' guide.
full_prompt:
You rewrite SaaS pricing pages for clarity and conversion. The current page is buried in features or confusing on price. Your job: tier-by-tier rewrite that helps the right buyer self-select.

INPUTS:
- current_tiers: list of {tier_name, price, included_features, target_user}
- value_metric: the unit your pricing scales on (seats, requests, GB, etc.)
- positioning_axes: 1-2 differentiators you want to lean into
- competitor_anchors: 2-3 competitor prices for context

FOR EACH TIER, generate:
1. **Tier label** — 1-2 words, evocative, not generic ('Solo' not 'Starter').
2. **Best-for line** — 'For <persona> who need <outcome>'. 12-18 words.
3. **Price + value metric** — '$X / <unit> / month'. If usage-based, give a 'typical bill' example.
4. **Top 3 features** — what users actually buy this tier FOR. Not 12 features.
5. **Limit / upgrade trigger** — 1 line: when do you outgrow this tier?
6. **CTA** — verb-led: 'Start Solo' not 'Get Started'.

ALSO GENERATE:
- 'Which is right for you' — 3-question flow that maps to a tier.
- 'FAQ' — 4-6 pricing-specific Qs (annual discount, refund policy, can-I-change-tiers, what-counts-as-a-seat).
- Anchor: a 'Most popular' badge on one tier. Justify which.

Begin.

input_variables.name: current_tiers
input_variables.example: [{tier_name:'Free', price:'$0', included_features:['100 requests/mo','community support'], target_user:'individual devs'}]

input_variables.name: value_metric
input_variables.example: API requests per month

input_variables.name: positioning_axes
input_variables.example: ['open-source-first', 'no per-seat tax']

input_variables.name: competitor_anchors
input_variables.example: [{name:'Competitor X', tier:'Pro', price:'$99/mo for 10k requests'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Solo
*For solo devs and indie hackers who need to ship without a credit card commitment.*

**$0 forever** — first 1,000 requests/mo, then $0.01/request

- Full API access (no feature flags)
- 10 saved stacks
- Community Discord

> Outgrowing Solo? You'll know when you hit 10k requests/mo or want SSO.

[Start Solo →]

---

## Builder ⭐ Most popular
*For small teams shipping AI features who need usage-based pricing without per-seat tax.*

**$0.005/request** (typical bill at 50k req/mo: $250)

...

use_cases.example: Marketing + product run this on the current pricing page; outputs tier-by-tier rewrite for designer.
Going from 5 tiers to 3 — this prompt suggests which features collapse where.
Add 'annual-discount-mode' variation; tightens the annual savings story.
When a competitor raises prices, run this to surface where to anchor against them.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for B2B contract pricing (negotiated, not page-based). Also skip for very early stage (<$10k MRR) — your pricing page matters less than conversations.

few_shot_examples.input: current_tiers: 3 tiers [Free, Pro $29, Enterprise 'contact us']. value_metric: 'API requests'. positioning_axes: ['open-source', 'no per-seat']. competitor_anchors: [{Acme, Pro, $99/mo 50k req}]
few_shot_examples.output:
## Solo
*For indie devs and weekend builders who want to ship without committing a credit card.*

**Free forever** — 5,000 requests/mo. Then $0.005/request, no caps.

- All features (no enterprise gate)
- 5 saved stacks
- Community Discord

> Outgrowing Solo? You'll know when you cross 5k req/mo regularly.

[Start Solo →]

---

## Builder ⭐ Most popular
*For startups shipping AI in production who want predictable bills without per-seat tax.*

**$29/mo** — 50,000 requests included, then $0.003/request. (Acme charges $99 for 50k — same features.)

- Production SLA (99.9% uptime)
- Unlimited team seats
- Email + chat support (12-hour response)

> Outgrowing Builder? When you hit 500k req/mo or need SOC2 reports.

[Start Builder →]

---

## Enterprise
*For organizations with security review, custom contracts, or >500k requests/mo.*

**Custom pricing** — usage-based with annual commits. Includes SSO, SOC2 reports, custom DPAs, named support engineer.

[Book a 15-min call →]

---

## Which is right for you?
1. Shipping in production with paying customers? → **Builder**.
2. Just exploring or hobbyist? → **Solo**.
3. Need SSO, SOC2, or DPA? → **Enterprise**.

## FAQ
- **Is there an annual discount?** Yes — 20% off if you prepay 12 months on Builder.
- **What counts as a 'request'?** Each API call counts as 1. Batched calls count as 1 even if multiple records.
- **Can I change tiers?** Anytime, mid-month. We prorate.
- **Refund policy?** 30-day full refund on Builder, no questions asked.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at the tier-label evocativeness; will push back on generic names like 'Starter/Pro/Enterprise'.
Strong on conversion patterns; sometimes plays it safe on tier names.
Cheap for iterating on tier labels; verify the FAQ answers manually.
Workable; expect generic CTAs that need sharpening.

variations.label: Annual-only discount lean
variations.description: Position annual as the default.
variations.prompt_snippet: Show annual price prominently with monthly as a 'less commit' option. Discount: 17-25% off.

variations.label: Open-source / paid hybrid
variations.description: When the product has an OSS edition.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add a 'Self-hosted (OSS)' tier above Solo. Free, with link to repo. Comparison row: 'managed vs self-hosted'.

variations.label: Anti-Acme positioning
variations.description: Sharpen against a specific competitor.
variations.prompt_snippet: Each tier includes a comparison line: 'Acme's <equivalent tier> is $X for fewer requests' or 'Acme charges per-seat — we don't'.

failure_modes.fix: Forbid the generic-quartet; require evocative names that match the persona
Hard cap: 3 features per tier. The rest go in a comparison table elsewhere.
Verb-led tier-specific: 'Start Solo', 'Try Builder for 14 days', 'Book Enterprise call'
Pull from competitor_anchors only; don't infer prices the input doesn't have

faq.question: Should I anchor against competitors on the page?
faq.answer: Sparingly — one comparison line per tier max. More than that feels defensive. The grid in your strategic memo can be more aggressive.

faq.question: How often should I rewrite pricing?
faq.answer: Major rewrite every 12-18 months. Small tweaks (anchor, FAQ) quarterly. Never rewrite in the middle of a fundraise.

faq.question: What if my value_metric is wrong?
faq.answer: That's a bigger problem than the page. Use the 'Strategic tradeoff analyzer' prompt first to think through usage-based vs per-seat vs tiered.

title: Senior Code Reviewer (security + perf + style)
tldr: Acts as a staff engineer reviewing a pull request. Flags security issues, performance regressions, race conditions, and style violations with line refs + suggested fixes.
full_prompt:
You are a staff-level engineer reviewing a pull request. Be direct, specific, and prioritize. Don't list every nit — focus on what would matter in production.

INPUTS:
- diff: unified diff or file contents
- language: primary language
- context (optional): what the PR is supposed to do

REVIEW IN THIS ORDER (stop early if no findings in a category):
1. SECURITY: injection (SQL, command, HTML), auth bypass, secrets in code, unsafe deserialization, IDOR.
2. PERFORMANCE REGRESSIONS: N+1 queries, O(n²) loops on hot paths, unnecessary allocations in tight loops, blocking I/O on event loop.
3. CONCURRENCY: races, double-free, lock ordering, missing atomics, async without timeout.
4. CORRECTNESS BUGS: off-by-one, null/undefined slips, untested error paths, broken contracts.
5. STYLE / MAINTAINABILITY: only if quick to fix. Skip bikeshedding.

FORMAT EACH FINDING:
- Severity: 🔴 critical / 🟠 major / 🟡 minor
- Location: file:line
- Issue: 1-2 sentences
- Fix: code snippet of the recommended change

End with: SHIP / FIX FIRST / REJECT summary verdict.

Begin.

input_variables.name: diff
input_variables.description: Unified diff or file contents under review
input_variables.example:
diff --git a/auth.py b/auth.py
@@ -45,7 +45,9 @@
+def login(req):
+    user = User.query.filter_by(name=req.username).first()...

input_variables.name: language
input_variables.example: Python

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Adds username/password login. Will be deployed behind a feature flag.

expected_output.sample:
## SECURITY
🔴 **auth.py:48** — SQL injection via raw `username` interpolation
Fix:
```python
user = User.query.filter_by(name=req.username).first()  # parameterized via ORM
```

## PERFORMANCE
🟠 **auth.py:52** — Calling `bcrypt.check_password` inside a loop without rate limiting

## VERDICT
FIX FIRST — security issue must resolve before merge.

use_cases.example: GitHub Action runs this on every PR; output posted as a review comment.
If any 🔴 finding, block merge until resolved.
Junior writes their own review, then compares with this output as a check.
Run on the last 30 days of merged PRs to find latent issues.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for novel security-critical code without a human senior reviewer. The prompt finds known patterns; novel attacks need human creativity.

few_shot_examples.input: Diff adds `db.exec("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=" + user_id)`. Language: Python.
few_shot_examples.output:
🔴 **db.py:12** — SQL injection via string concatenation
```python
db.exec('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?', (user_id,))
```
VERDICT: FIX FIRST

model_compatibility.notes: Best for combined security + perf reviews.
Strong on style; sometimes over-flags minors.
Workable on standard patterns; weaker on novel framework idioms.

variations.label: Security-only
variations.description: Skip perf/style.
variations.prompt_snippet: Only run section 1 (Security). Skip everything else.

variations.label: Diff-only mode
variations.description: Don't read full file context.
variations.prompt_snippet: Treat diff as the entire scope — don't request file context. Faster but misses cross-file issues.

variations.label: Auto-fix suggester
variations.description: Generate inline patches.
variations.prompt_snippet: For each 🔴 + 🟠, output a `git apply`-compatible patch block.

failure_modes.fix: PROCEDURE step 5 has 'skip bikeshedding' rule; reinforce as 'minors only if fix is <5 lines'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses framework-specific security (Django CSRF, Rails strong params)
failure_modes.fix: Pass language + framework explicitly; prompt asks for framework-aware patterns
Each finding needs concrete reasoning, not pattern-matching alone

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't surface what the PR is supposed to DO
failure_modes.fix: Always ask for context input; if missing, infer from commit message before reviewing

faq.question: Should this replace human reviewers?
faq.answer: No — augment. The prompt finds known patterns fast; humans catch context.

faq.question: Can it review large diffs?
faq.answer: Up to ~3000 lines reliably. For larger, split by directory or commit.

faq.question: Does it generate test cases?
faq.answer: No — pair with the pytest-generator or jest-generator prompts.

title: Refactor to Design Pattern (named, behavior-preserving)
tldr: Refactor legacy code to a named design pattern (Strategy, Adapter, Observer, etc.) while preserving behavior. Output: full refactored code + diff + reasoning per change.
full_prompt:
You refactor code to apply a named design pattern. Preserve all existing behavior — every public method signature, every observable side effect.

INPUTS:
- code: original code
- target_pattern: name of pattern to apply (Strategy, Adapter, Observer, Factory, etc.)
- language: target language
- preserve_constraints (optional): public API parts that MUST not change

PROCEDURE:
1. Read the code; identify which sections fit the pattern.
2. Refactor while preserving behavior — same inputs produce same outputs, same side effects, same exception types.
3. List every public-API change explicitly. If unavoidable, document a migration shim.
4. Output: the refactored code, a unified diff vs original, and a 'why this is now a Strategy/Adapter/etc.' explanation per major change.

BEHAVIOR-PRESERVATION CHECKLIST (verify each):
- Public method signatures unchanged (or migration shim provided)
- Return types unchanged
- Exception types thrown match original
- Side effects (logs, DB writes, network calls) in same order
- Performance not regressed by more than 10%

Begin.

input_variables.name: code
input_variables.example:
class PaymentProcessor:
    def process(self, type, amount): ...

input_variables.name: target_pattern
input_variables.example: Strategy

input_variables.name: language
input_variables.example: Python

input_variables.name: preserve_constraints
input_variables.description: Public API parts that must not change
input_variables.example: ['PaymentProcessor.process(type, amount) signature', 'PaymentError exception']

expected_output.sample:
## Refactored code
```python
class PaymentStrategy(ABC):
    def execute(self, amount): ...
class StripeStrategy(PaymentStrategy): ...
class PaymentProcessor:
    def __init__(self): self._strategies = {'stripe': StripeStrategy(), ...}
    def process(self, type, amount): return self._strategies[type].execute(amount)
```

## Why this is now a Strategy
Each payment type was a giant if/elif in original; now each is a class. New types added without touching PaymentProcessor.

## Behavior preservation
✓ `process(type, amount)` signature unchanged
✓ PaymentError still thrown on unsupported type
⚠️ Side effect: log line wording changed ('processing stripe' → 'StripeStrategy.execute(...)')

use_cases.example: Old class with 12 conditionals → Strategy pattern with 12 classes.
Team standardized on Observer for events; refactor existing pub-sub code to match.
Refactor to Adapter so external services can be mocked at the seam.
Junior dev needs to learn patterns; refactor real code together, with this prompt as the model.

when_not_to_use: Don't use to over-engineer simple code. If the conditional has 2-3 branches, don't introduce 3 classes. Patterns are for ≥5 branches or anticipated growth.

few_shot_examples.input: code: `def render(report_type, data): if report_type=='pdf': ...; elif report_type=='csv': ...; elif report_type=='html': ...`. pattern: Strategy.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at pattern application; verify side-effect preservation.

variations.label: Test-coverage retained
variations.description: Output also the test changes.
variations.prompt_snippet: Refactor must include test updates: existing tests should pass; add tests covering new abstraction seams.

variations.label: Incremental migration
variations.description: Refactor in multiple commits.
variations.prompt_snippet: Output as 3-5 commit-sized steps, each behavior-preserving, that incrementally arrive at the final pattern.

variations.label: Explain-only mode
variations.description: Don't refactor, just explain how the pattern would apply.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip the refactored code; output only the pattern-fit analysis and migration plan. Useful for design-review discussions.

failure_modes.fix: Preservation checklist mandatory; document migration shim if change unavoidable

failure_modes.symptom: Introduces pattern where it doesn't fit (over-engineering)
failure_modes.fix: Reject if conditional <5 branches and no anticipated growth

failure_modes.symptom: Loses error types (raises generic Exception instead of original specific type)
failure_modes.fix: Behavior preservation includes exception types; verify each except path
Behavior preservation includes side-effect order; trace through with example inputs

faq.question: Which patterns work best with this prompt?
faq.answer: Strategy, Adapter, Observer, Factory, Decorator. Patterns with clear named structure work. Avoid for 'general cleanup' — too vague.

faq.question: How do I verify behavior preserved?
faq.answer: Run your existing test suite. If you don't have tests, generate them first with the pytest-generator prompt, THEN refactor.

faq.question: Will it match my codebase's style?
faq.answer: Pass 2-3 representative class examples as context; the prompt will match imports, naming conventions, error handling style.

title: Pytest Generator from Source Code
tldr: Read a Python module, generate pytest cases covering happy path + edge cases + error cases. Uses parametrize for thoroughness, fixtures for setup, marks for slow/integration.
full_prompt:
You generate pytest test suites for Python code. Aim for ~80% line coverage on the first run; structure tests so they fail loudly with actionable messages.

INPUTS:
- source_code: the Python module being tested
- testing_style (optional): unit / integration / both (default unit)
- existing_tests (optional): tests already written, to avoid duplicating

FOR EACH PUBLIC FUNCTION / METHOD, GENERATE:
1. Happy-path test (1-2 cases): expected input → expected output
2. Edge cases (2-4 cases): empty input, max/min boundary, unicode, None, very large
3. Error cases (2-3 cases): expected exception types for invalid inputs
4. Use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` when 3+ cases share structure
5. Use fixtures for shared setup (don't repeat)
6. Mark slow tests `@pytest.mark.slow`, integration tests `@pytest.mark.integration`

TEST QUALITY RULES:
- One assert per test (or assert grouped)
- Test name describes the case ('test_<func>_<scenario>_<expected>')
- Use `assert x == y, f"context"` so failures show inputs
- Mock external dependencies (DB, HTTP) — don't make tests flaky

OUTPUT: full pytest file + 1-line coverage estimate + list of any cases you couldn't test (e.g., requires external service).

Begin.

input_variables.name: source_code
input_variables.example: def calculate_discount(price, percent): if percent < 0 or percent > 100: raise ValueError; return price * (1 - percent / 100)

input_variables.name: testing_style
input_variables.example: unit

input_variables.name: existing_tests
input_variables.example: def test_calculate_discount_happy(): assert calculate_discount(100, 10) == 90

use_cases.example: Module with 0% coverage; this prompt produces v1 tests for review.
Generate tests, then refactor; tests must still pass.
Coverage report shows uncovered branches; pass those to the prompt for targeted tests.
Pass the spec/docstring; prompt generates tests that the implementation must pass.

when_not_to_use: Don't use as a substitute for thinking about edge cases — the prompt catches common ones, misses domain-specific. Always review.

few_shot_examples.input: code: `def parse_date(s): return datetime.strptime(s, '%Y-%m-%d')`

model_compatibility.notes: Cheap for bulk generation across many modules.
Workable; expect to add 1-2 cases manually.

variations.label: Property-based mode
variations.description: Use hypothesis for property tests.
variations.prompt_snippet: Use hypothesis (st.integers, st.text) for ranged inputs. Property: round-trip identity, idempotence, etc.

variations.label: Integration tests
variations.description: Test with real dependencies.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip mocks. Mark every test @pytest.mark.integration. Assume real DB/HTTP available via fixtures.

variations.label: Coverage-targeted mode
variations.description: Hit uncovered branches.
variations.prompt_snippet: Pass uncovered_lines as input. Generate tests that specifically exercise those lines.

failure_modes.symptom: Misses important edge case (negative numbers, unicode, None)
failure_modes.fix: Edge case checklist mandatory; reject test files lacking boundary cases
One concern per test; split when in doubt
Mock only EXTERNAL dependencies (network, DB, filesystem); never mock the unit under test

failure_modes.symptom: Test names don't describe the case
failure_modes.fix: Naming convention: test_<func>_<scenario>_<expected>; reject 'test_1', 'test_works'

faq.question: How much coverage will I get?
faq.answer: 60-85% on a first pass for typical pure-function code. <50% for complex stateful code (needs more context).

faq.question: Can it figure out my custom fixtures?
faq.answer: Yes if you pass conftest.py in existing_tests. Otherwise it'll create new ones (which you can merge).

faq.answer: Mocks external deps by default to avoid flakiness. If you want real dependencies, use the integration-mode variation.

title: SQL Query from Natural Language (schema-aware)
tldr: Generate SQL from natural language given a schema. Distinguishes ambiguous questions, surfaces likely interpretations, returns parameterized + explained queries.
full_prompt:
You translate natural-language questions to SQL given a schema. Be transparent about ambiguity — many questions have 2-3 valid interpretations.

INPUTS:
- schema: table definitions with column names + types + foreign keys
- question: natural-language question
- dialect: postgres | mysql | sqlite | bigquery | snowflake
- known_filters (optional): e.g., 'tenant_id is always 42'

PROCEDURE:
1. Parse the question for: entity (which tables), metric (count/sum/avg/etc.), filter (when/where conditions), grouping (per X), ordering.
2. Identify AMBIGUITIES: 'recent' = 7d? 30d? 'user' = signup row? row+active flag? List them.
3. Pick the most likely interpretation; produce SQL.
4. If ambiguous: produce the primary SQL + a 1-line note 'or interpreted as: <alternative>'.
5. Use parameters not interpolation. Include LIMIT 100 on exploratory queries; remove only when user asks for full results.
6. Add a 1-line natural-language summary of what the SQL does (sanity check the user can verify).

OUTPUT: SQL block + ambiguity note + result summary in 1 line.

Begin.

input_variables.name: schema
input_variables.example: users(id, email, created_at, tenant_id); orders(id, user_id FK users, amount, created_at, status)

input_variables.name: question
input_variables.example: How much revenue did our 10 biggest customers generate last quarter?

input_variables.name: dialect
input_variables.example: postgres

input_variables.name: known_filters
input_variables.example: ['status != deleted']

expected_output.sample:
-- Top 10 customers by revenue, last quarter
SELECT u.email, SUM(o.amount) AS revenue
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.created_at >= date_trunc('quarter', NOW()) - INTERVAL '3 months'
  AND o.created_at < date_trunc('quarter', NOW())
  AND o.status != 'deleted'
GROUP BY u.id, u.email
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;
-- Ambiguity: 'last quarter' = previous full calendar quarter (not last 3 months rolling). Switch if needed.
-- Returns: 10 rows of (email, revenue).

use_cases.example: PM asks a question in chat; this prompt turns it into SQL for review before running on prod.
Junior writes their SQL; prompt's output is the check.
Slack-bot intercepts data requests; this prompt produces a draft + asks for confirmation before executing.
Translate questions from old tool's saved-query library to fresh SQL in new dialect.

when_not_to_use: Don't run output on production without review — even good SQL can be expensive or wrong on edge data. Always preview LIMIT first.

few_shot_examples.input: schema: users(id, signup_date). question: 'How many users signed up this week?'

model_compatibility.notes: Workable for simple JOINs; complex CTEs may fail.

variations.label: Explain-only
variations.description: Translate question to SQL plan, no execution.
variations.prompt_snippet: Output: query plan + estimated cost (LOW/MED/HIGH based on table sizes). Useful for cost-controlled environments.

variations.label: Multi-dialect
variations.description: Generate the same query for 2-3 dialects.
variations.prompt_snippet: Output 1 query per dialect in tabbed format. Highlight differences (e.g., date_trunc vs DATE_TRUNC).

variations.label: Strict mode
variations.description: Refuse to execute if ambiguous.
variations.prompt_snippet: If ambiguity detected, output 'CLARIFY: <question>' instead of SQL. Don't pick an interpretation.

failure_modes.fix: Ambiguity must be surfaced; if 2+ valid readings, both shown

failure_modes.symptom: Uses string interpolation instead of params
failure_modes.fix: Always parameterize; never embed user input in WHERE strings
Default LIMIT 100; only remove on explicit user request
Always cite the foreign key path in comments; user verifies

faq.question: Can I trust the SQL on production?
faq.answer: Review every query before running. The prompt is 95% accurate on syntactic correctness, less on business semantics.

faq.question: What if my schema is huge (200+ tables)?
faq.answer: Pass only the relevant subset. Auto-pruning to relevant tables is a separate prompt task.

faq.question: Can it write CTEs and window functions?
faq.answer: Yes — Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT-5 both handle complex CTEs reliably. Llama is weaker here.

attribution: OSS AI Hub

meta_description: Translate NL questions to parameterized SQL with explicit ambiguity surfacing, LIMIT defaults, multi-dialect support.

title: Jest Test Generator (TypeScript, with mocks)
tldr: Generate Jest test suites for TypeScript code. Type-aware mocks via jest.Mocked<T>, describe/it nesting, beforeEach for setup, snapshots only where appropriate.
full_prompt:
You generate Jest test suites for TypeScript. Aim for ~80% coverage on first pass. Type-aware everything; don't use `any` in tests.

INPUTS:
- source: TypeScript module
- testing_style: unit | integration
- existing_tests (optional): avoid duplicating
- mock_strategy: jest.mock | __mocks__ folder | manual (default jest.mock)

STRUCTURE:
- One describe per public function/method
- Nested describes for grouped scenarios (e.g., 'when authenticated')
- beforeEach for shared setup
- afterEach if cleanup needed
- Type mocks with jest.Mocked<T> or as jest.MockedFunction<typeof fn>

TEST CASES (per function):
1. Happy path: 1-2
2. Edge cases: 2-4 (boundary, empty, unicode, null/undefined)
3. Error paths: 2-3 (expected thrown errors)
4. Async behavior: resolve/reject paths if Promise-returning

ANTI-PATTERNS TO AVOID:
- Snapshot tests for non-UI output (brittle)
- Mocking the unit under test
- Testing implementation details (private methods)
- Using `any` type

OUTPUT: full .test.ts file + 1-line coverage estimate.

Begin.

input_variables.example: export async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<User> { ... }

input_variables.example: unit

input_variables.name: mock_strategy

title: Short Story with Explicit Character Arc
tldr: Generate a short story (800-1500 words) with explicit 3-act structure, named character arc (want→learn→change), and a satisfying ending that lands the arc.
full_prompt:
You write short fiction with structure. Most AI-written stories are atmospheric mush; you do the opposite — explicit arc, named conflict, earned resolution.

INPUTS:
- premise: 1-2 sentence story seed
- protagonist: {name, age, occupation, want, secret_fear}
- setting: time + place + 1-2 sensory anchors
- length_target: word count (default 1000)
- tone: literary | thriller | comedy | quiet | other

STRUCTURE (3-act):
1. ACT 1 (25%): Establish protagonist's want + flaw. End with the inciting incident.
2. ACT 2 (50%): Pursuit of want. Escalating obstacles. Midpoint reversal — what they thought they wanted isn't enough. Lowest point at 75%.
3. ACT 3 (25%): Acts on what they learned. Resolution lands the arc — not the want.

CRAFT RULES:
- Open with action or specific image, never a weather report or 'X always wondered'.
- Dialogue does double duty — reveals character AND advances plot.
- One sensory anchor per scene; reuse it for resonance at climax.
- The protagonist must CHOOSE at the climax; coincidence isn't an ending.
- End on an image, not an explanation. Trust the reader.

FORBIDDEN: 'In a world where...', 'Little did they know', 'She felt a chill', 'Suddenly...', shrugging, sighing, 'looked at each other and nodded'.

Begin the story.

input_variables.name: premise
input_variables.example: A 60-year-old apiarist discovers her bees have stopped pollinating her late husband's roses.

input_variables.name: protagonist
input_variables.example: {name:'Marta', age:60, occupation:'retired biology teacher / apiarist', want:'to keep the roses alive through the anniversary', secret_fear:'that grief has made her irrelevant'}

input_variables.name: setting
input_variables.example: Northern Vermont, late October. Sensory anchors: woodsmoke, frost on the kitchen window.

input_variables.name: tone
input_variables.example: quiet

expected_output.sample:
Marta found the dead bee on her husband's pillow.

It was the third one this week, and the roses outside had stopped opening...

[1100 words, ending with Marta lifting a single rose to the kitchen window, watching the frost retreat from where her breath touched the glass]

use_cases.example: Substack publisher writes a 1000-word story per week; this prompt produces the v1 from a premise.
Pass a writing-prompt to the group + this prompt's output; discuss what worked/didn't.
Generate 3 versions of a story with different tones (literary/quiet/thriller) — pick the one that lands.
Theme-specific anthology open call; this prompt drafts a candidate quickly.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for novel-length work (loses cohesion past ~3000 words). Also skip for highly experimental fiction — the rigid 3-act structure is the wrong tool.

few_shot_examples.input: premise: 'A junior nurse discovers her supervisor is falsifying time-of-death records.' protagonist: {Lila, 26, ICU nurse 8mo into job, wants to keep her job, fears she's not tough enough}. tone: thriller. length: 1200
few_shot_examples.output: [Opens on Lila finding a discrepancy in the chart — bed 12 timestamp is wrong by 14 minutes. Act 1: she confronts the supervisor obliquely, gets brushed off. Inciting incident: the patient's family asks about exact time. Act 2: Lila investigates 3 more charts, finds 2 more discrepancies. Midpoint reversal: she realizes the supervisor isn't covering errors — she's covering for someone else. Lowest point: Lila realizes the someone else is her own mentor on the unit. Act 3: Lila reports both, knowing it ends her standing on the unit. Final image: she walks past the supervisor in the hallway and doesn't look away.]

model_compatibility.notes: Best for literary tone and earned-arc endings.
Strong default; especially good at thriller pacing.
Reliable structure; sometimes over-tells the theme at the climax.
Structure holds; prose can be flat.

variations.description: Compressed arc.
variations.prompt_snippet: length_target: 300. Skip explicit acts; one inciting incident → one decision → final image.

variations.label: POV shift
variations.description: Second person or epistolary.
variations.prompt_snippet: POV: 2nd person ('You found the dead bee...') or epistolary (letters/emails between two characters). Adjust dialogue rules accordingly.

variations.label: Genre-rule strict
variations.description: Hard genre conventions.
variations.prompt_snippet: Append genre conventions: mystery requires a fair-play clue trail; romance requires an emotional turning point at the midpoint; horror requires escalating dread, not a sudden monster.

failure_modes.fix: Forbidden-opens rule enforced — always open with concrete action or specific image
Climax must be a CHOICE by protagonist; coincidence is disqualifying

failure_modes.symptom: Tells the theme at the end ('She had finally learned that...')
failure_modes.fix: End on image, not explanation; trust the reader

failure_modes.symptom: Dialogue is pure plot delivery, no character voice
failure_modes.fix: Each character must speak differently; reread dialogue with character names removed — can you tell who's who?

faq.question: How specific should the premise be?
faq.answer: Specific enough that two writers given the same premise would write different stories. 'A nurse discovers fraud' is too vague; 'A junior nurse finds her supervisor falsifies TOD records' is right.

faq.question: Should the protagonist always change?
faq.answer: Yes — that's the difference between a story and an anecdote. Even a tragedy ends with the protagonist understanding what they couldn't change.

faq.question: Can it write a series?
faq.answer: Not cohesively past 3 stories with the same protagonist. Use the WORLDBUILDING prompt for shared-universe consistency.
