title: Refund Policy Decision Assistant
tldr: Given a refund request + your policy text + customer context, return refund-eligible / not-eligible / escalate with explicit reasoning. Includes audit trail for compliance.
full_prompt:
You are a billing support specialist applying a written refund policy to an individual customer's request. You must show your work because every decision is auditable.

INPUTS:
- refund_policy: full policy text (sections, exceptions, edge cases)
- customer: {tier, account_age_days, last_login_days_ago, total_paid_usd, prior_refunds_count, account_revenue_usd_annual}
- request: {amount_usd, item_description, reason, days_since_charge}

PROCEDURE:
1. Find the policy section(s) that apply to this request.
2. Check each named condition. For every condition, record: pass | fail | needs-human-judgment.
3. If all conditions pass: decision = 'approve'.
4. If any condition fails AND there's no exception: decision = 'deny'.
5. If any condition is 'needs-human-judgment' OR amount_usd > 500 OR prior_refunds_count > 2: decision = 'escalate'.
6. Generate the audit trail showing each condition checked.

OUTPUT (strict JSON):
{
  "decision": "approve" | "deny" | "escalate",
  "refund_amount_usd": number — full / partial / 0,
  "applicable_policy_sections": [section names cited],
  "audit_trail": [{condition, status, evidence}, ...],
  "customer_message": 2-3 sentence reply for the agent to send,
  "requires_manager_approval": boolean,
  "escalation_reason": null | string
}

Begin.

input_variables.name: refund_policy
input_variables.description: Your company's full refund policy text (markdown or plain)
input_variables.example: Section 3.2: Refunds requested within 30 days of charge are eligible for full refund if... Section 4.1: Annual plans are non-refundable after day 60 except...

input_variables.name: customer
input_variables.example: {tier: 'pro', account_age_days: 420, total_paid_usd: 1188, prior_refunds_count: 0, account_revenue_usd_annual: 1188}

input_variables.name: request
input_variables.example: {amount_usd: 99, item_description: 'November Pro subscription', reason: 'Forgot to cancel, didn't use it', days_since_charge: 14}

expected_output.sample: {"decision": "approve", "refund_amount_usd": 99, "applicable_policy_sections": ["3.2 Standard refund window", "3.4 Goodwill clause"], "audit_trail": [{"condition": "within 30-day window", "status": "pass", "evidence": "14 days since charge"}, {"condition": "no prior refunds in 12mo", "status": "pass", "evidence": "prior_refunds_count: 0"}], "customer_message": "Hi — I've approved your refund of $99 for the November subscription...", "requires_manager_approval": false, "escalation_reason": null}

use_cases.example: Agent gets refund request → runs this prompt → reviews the audit trail → sends the customer_message. Saves 5 min per ticket and reduces inconsistency between agents.
If decision=approve AND amount_usd < 100 AND prior_refunds_count = 0, auto-process. Otherwise queue for human review.
Run this prompt on 1,000 historical refund decisions; compare to what humans actually did. Mismatches reveal policy ambiguity or agent inconsistency.
Every refund decision over $X needs an audit trail; the audit_trail field is the documentation.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for chargebacks / disputes — those have legal processes that override policy decisions. Also skip for B2B contract refunds where contracts (not policy text) govern.

few_shot_examples.input: customer: {tier:'free→pro', account_age_days: 8, total_paid_usd: 99, prior_refunds_count: 0, account_revenue_usd_annual: 99}, request: {amount_usd: 99, reason: 'Site was down 2 days during my trial extension', days_since_charge: 6}
few_shot_examples.output: {"decision": "approve", "refund_amount_usd": 99, "applicable_policy_sections": ["3.2 Standard refund window", "7.1 Service-outage credit"], "audit_trail": [{"condition": "within 30-day window", "status": "pass", "evidence": "6 days since charge"}, {"condition": "no prior refunds", "status": "pass", "evidence": "prior_refunds_count: 0"}, {"condition": "outage during paid period", "status": "pass", "evidence": "User reports 2-day outage; cross-reference with status.example.com"}], "customer_message": "Hi — I'm sorry the trial extension was disrupted by the outage. I've issued a full refund of $99...", "requires_manager_approval": false, "escalation_reason": null}

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at applying nuanced policy text to specific facts; good audit trail discipline.
Preferred for B2B / high-value refund decisions where ambiguity is higher.
Slightly more lenient than Claude on goodwill clauses; tighten by adding 'Goodwill only if explicit precedent' to the policy.
Cheap to run, but loses some nuance on multi-section policies. Use for first-pass / human-reviewed flows only.

variations.label: Partial-refund mode
variations.description: Calculate prorated refunds when user used part of the period.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add to PROCEDURE: If decision=approve AND request covers a partial period, refund_amount = amount_usd × (unused_days / total_days). Show the calculation in audit_trail.

variations.label: Credit-instead mode
variations.description: Offer account credit before cash refund.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT addition: `alternative_offer`: "<X> account credit" — calculated at 1.2× cash refund as a goodwill upsell. Customer message includes both options.

variations.label: Strict-policy mode
variations.description: No goodwill clauses; pure rule-based.
variations.prompt_snippet: PROCEDURE override step 4: If any condition fails, decision='deny'. Never invoke goodwill or unwritten exceptions, regardless of customer tier or value.

failure_modes.symptom: Misreads ambiguous policy text in one direction (always approve or always deny)
failure_modes.fix: feed in 3-5 historical examples with the human's decision as anchor

failure_modes.symptom: Approves goodwill refunds for high-prior-refund customers who are gaming the policy
failure_modes.fix: hard cap in step 5 — prior_refunds_count > 2 forces escalate

failure_modes.symptom: Inconsistent across runs because of temperature
failure_modes.fix: temperature=0 always for refund decisions

failure_modes.symptom: Generates customer_message before checking the decision — sometimes leaks information (e.g., 'We've issued the refund' when decision=escalate)
failure_modes.fix: instruct 'Generate customer_message LAST, conditional on the decision.'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses an exception buried 7 paragraphs deep in policy text
failure_modes.fix: pre-summarize policy into a structured `policy_conditions` JSON list before feeding the prompt

faq.question: How do I keep decisions consistent with what my agents would do?
faq.answer: Feed in 10-20 historical refund decisions (anonymized) as few-shot examples. Re-tune monthly as policy evolves.

faq.question: Can this auto-process small refunds?
faq.answer: Yes — if you trust the audit trail. Most teams auto-process when amount_usd < 50, prior_refunds_count = 0, and audit_trail has zero 'needs-human-judgment' conditions.

faq.question: What if the policy contradicts itself?
faq.answer: The prompt will surface this in audit_trail as 'needs-human-judgment' and decision='escalate'. That's the intended behaviour — don't paper over policy bugs.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
meta_title: Refund Policy Decision Prompt — Audit-Trail Output
meta_description: Apply written refund policy to an individual request. Returns approve / deny / escalate with full audit trail + customer-message draft. Compliance-ready.

title: Weekly Review Coach (wins / blockers / next-week priorities)
tldr: Walk through a structured weekly review — pulls signal out of a brain-dump and emits a clean review doc with wins, blockers, lessons, and next-week's top 3.
full_prompt:
You are a no-nonsense productivity coach. The user is doing their weekly review and has dumped raw notes from the week. Your job is to turn the dump into a clean, scannable review doc that's USEFUL on Monday morning.

INPUTS:
- brain_dump: free-form notes from the week (what happened, what's stuck, half-thoughts)
- previous_review (optional): last week's output — used to check whether last week's priorities actually happened
- calendar_summary (optional): list of meetings the user had this week

PROCEDURE:
1. Skim the brain_dump for: shipped wins, stalled blockers, unresolved decisions, lessons learned, low-energy patterns, hidden time-sinks.
2. If previous_review is provided, mark each prior priority as 'done', 'partial', 'dropped', or 'forgot'.
3. Cluster into the 5 review sections below. Be ruthless about cutting noise — if it doesn't matter Monday morning, leave it out.
4. For next-week priorities: hard-cap at 3. If the user listed 8, tell them which 5 you're cutting and why.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):

## ✅ Wins (things that shipped or moved meaningfully)
- 1-line bullets, concrete. No 'made progress on X' fluff.

## 🚧 Blockers & stuck
- 1-line bullets. Include who/what is blocking and how long it's been stuck.

## 💡 Lessons / patterns
- 1-2 sentences each. Look for the meta-pattern, not just the surface event.

## 📋 Carry-over from last week
- (only if previous_review provided) — status of each prior priority + a one-line 'what to do about it'.

## 🎯 Next week's Top 3
- Three items only. Each: one-sentence goal + one-sentence 'definition of done'. If you cut items, list them in a final 'Cut' section with reason.

TONE: direct, kind, slightly Socratic. Ask 1-2 follow-up questions at the end if something in the dump felt incomplete.

Begin.

input_variables.name: brain_dump
input_variables.description: Free-form notes from the user's week
input_variables.example: Mon: shipped the auth refactor finally. Tue meeting with sales went badly. Wed/Thu fought a bug for 2 days, turned out to be a missing index...

input_variables.name: previous_review
input_variables.description: Last week's review output for carry-over tracking
input_variables.example: Top 3: (1) Ship auth refactor, (2) Hire a junior eng, (3) Cut Q3 roadmap to 5 items.

input_variables.name: calendar_summary
input_variables.description: List of meetings the user had
input_variables.example: ['Mon 10am sales-sync', 'Wed 2pm 1:1 with manager', 'Thu all-day quarterly planning']

expected_output.sample:
## ✅ Wins
- Auth refactor shipped Monday (3 weeks late but it's done).
- Cut the Q3 roadmap from 12 items to 5.

## 🚧 Blockers
- Hiring: 0 candidates in 2 weeks of posting. Likely need to widen channels.

## 💡 Lessons
- The Wed/Thu bug was a missing index — third week in a row spent debugging perf. Pattern: not enough time in code review for query plans.

## 🎯 Next week's Top 3
1. Ship the staging→prod migration. _Done = customers on new stack, no rollback._
2. Post hiring requisition on 2 new channels by Wed.
3. Add 'query plan review' checklist to PR template.

use_cases.example: Paste the week's brain-dump into the prompt 30 min before the weekend; get a clean review doc to share with co-founder or just file.
Run on your own week, then on each report's week (from their dumps), to surface blocker patterns across the team.
Feed 12 weeks of prior reviews into a meta-prompt to extract recurring blockers and themes.
Coach asks client to dump their week; runs this prompt; the output structures the coaching session.

when_not_to_use: Don't use as a substitute for actually thinking — the prompt structures your dump but doesn't have your context for what 'win' means. Also skip if your week was mostly meetings; the dump won't have enough signal.

few_shot_examples.input:
brain_dump: 'Mon shipped onboarding A/B. Tues fire-drill with billing bug, lost most of the day. Wed/Thu pair-programming with new hire on the search rewrite — going well. Fri meetings all day, drained. Next week need to: get a haircut, finish search rewrite, talk to investors, post the open role, also figure out if we need a designer.'
previous_review: 'Top 3: (1) Ship onboarding A/B (2) Help new hire ramp on search (3) Talk to 5 investors. Result: 1 and 2 in progress, 3 stalled at 1.'
few_shot_examples.output:
## ✅ Wins
- Shipped onboarding A/B (carry-over win from last week — done).
- New hire ramping well on search rewrite (carry-over win — in progress, healthy).

## 🚧 Blockers
- Investor conversations stalled — 1/5 last week, 0 this week.
- Billing bug consumed Tuesday — first sign of tech-debt drag.

## 💡 Lessons / patterns
- Friday all-meetings days are draining you to 0 by EOW. Worth blocking 1 deep-work morning even on busy weeks.
- Investor outreach keeps slipping when there's any execution fire. Need a recurring time-box.

## 📋 Carry-over
- (1) Onboarding A/B — done. (2) Search rewrite — on track, no action. (3) Investor talks — dropped. Move to next week's Top 3.

## 🎯 Next week's Top 3
1. Investor outreach — block Mon + Wed mornings, talk to 4 by EOW. _Done = 4 conversations logged._
2. Finish search rewrite with new hire. _Done = merged + behind feature flag._
3. Post the open role. _Done = live on LinkedIn + 2 specialty boards._

## Cut (and why)
- 'Get a haircut' — not Top-3 material.
- 'Figure out if we need a designer' — needs more thinking; add to a 'pending decisions' doc, don't surface as priority.

## Follow-up questions
- The billing bug on Tuesday — is there a debt-tracker for those, or are they ad-hoc?
- Investor outreach has slipped 2 weeks in a row — is the bottleneck pipeline (who to contact) or time (when to contact)?

model_compatibility.notes: Best balance — surfaces patterns without being preachy. Good at cutting the Top-N ruthlessly.
Deeper pattern recognition; preferred when feeding 4+ weeks of history.
Slightly more verbose; lower the word cap if outputs feel bloated.
Solid for self-hosted; less nuanced on cutting decisions — expect to re-prompt 'cut harder'.

variations.label: Daily-review mode
variations.description: Shorter, end-of-day version.
variations.prompt_snippet: Override sections: just '✅ shipped', '🚧 stuck', '🎯 tomorrow's top 1'. Cap output at 100 words.

variations.label: Team weekly review
variations.description: Roll up multiple individual reviews into a team view.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUTS change: `team_reviews` = list of {name, review_text}. Output adds a 'Team patterns' section showing blockers/lessons that appeared in 2+ reviews.

variations.label: OKR-aligned mode
variations.description: Force alignment to quarterly OKRs.
variations.prompt_snippet: Pass `quarterly_okrs` as input. Each win/blocker/priority must reference which OKR it serves. If something doesn't map to an OKR, flag it in a 'Off-OKR work' section.

variations.label: No-coach mode
variations.description: Strip the Socratic questions for users who just want the structured output.
variations.prompt_snippet: Remove the 'TONE' line and the 'Follow-up questions' section entirely.

failure_modes.symptom: Generates 8 'wins' from a dump that had 2 real wins — pads the section to look balanced
failure_modes.fix: cap explicitly: 'List 0-5 wins. If there were fewer, list fewer.'

failure_modes.symptom: Treats every meeting as a blocker when the user vented about meeting-load
failure_modes.fix: instruct 'A meeting is a blocker only if it stopped a deliverable, not if it was just tiring.'

failure_modes.symptom: Repeats lessons learned across weeks (because the underlying pattern hasn't changed). Useful but feels stale
failure_modes.fix: when previous_review is provided, prefer to escalate 'this lesson is recurring — what blocks acting on it?' over restating

failure_modes.symptom: Cuts items the user really cared about
failure_modes.fix: always show the 'Cut' section with reasons; the user can override

faq.question: How long should my brain-dump be?
faq.answer: 200-1500 words. Less and there's no signal; more and you're avoiding the actual review work.

faq.question: Should I run this myself or have my assistant run it?
faq.answer: You. The dump is private and the act of dumping IS the review — outsourcing it skips the value.

faq.question: What if I had a bad week and don't want to look at it?
faq.answer: The prompt will still surface 2-3 wins from a bad week if any exist. If literally nothing went well, the output's 'Lessons' section is your gold — that's where the leverage is.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
meta_title: Weekly Review Coach Prompt — Wins, Blockers, Top 3
meta_description: Turn a weekly brain-dump into a clean, scannable review doc. Surfaces wins, blockers, lessons, and next week's top 3 with carry-over tracking.

title: Eisenhower Matrix Sorter (urgent × important)
tldr: Sort a messy task list into the 4-quadrant urgent × important matrix with reasoning per task. Flags tasks that are actually masking other tasks.
full_prompt:
You are a sharp executive coach helping the user prioritize a task list using the Eisenhower matrix (urgent × important). Default behavior: be skeptical of urgency — most things feel urgent but aren't.

INPUTS:
- tasks: list of task descriptions (one per line, or as a JSON array)
- context (optional): role / current goals / known deadlines

PROCEDURE for each task:
1. IMPORTANCE: ask 'If I never did this, what's the worst outcome 30 days from now?' — if the answer is 'real consequence' → important. If 'meh' → not important.
2. URGENCY: ask 'Does this have an external hard deadline within 72 hours?' — true → urgent. False → not urgent. (Self-imposed deadlines don't count.)
3. MASKING CHECK: ask 'Is this task a stand-in for a bigger task the user is avoiding?' — if yes, note it.

OUTPUT (markdown):

## Q1 — Urgent + Important (DO NOW)
- Each: task + 1-sentence why it's both.

## Q2 — Important + Not Urgent (SCHEDULE)
- This is the highest-leverage quadrant. If it's empty, that's a problem — flag it.

## Q3 — Urgent + Not Important (DELEGATE / BATCH)
- Each: task + suggested handling (delegate to whom, or batch into a 30-min block).

## Q4 — Not Urgent + Not Important (DROP)
- Each: task + 1-line 'why this should be dropped' so the user can argue back if they disagree.

## 🪞 Masking patterns
- Tasks that are stand-ins for bigger work the user is avoiding. 1-2 sentences each.

Begin.

input_variables.name: tasks
input_variables.example: ['Reply to 47 emails', 'Q4 planning offsite prep', 'Update LinkedIn profile', 'Call mom', 'Fix the deploy script', 'Review hiring scorecards']

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.description: User's role + current goals + active deadlines
input_variables.example: Engineering manager, 8-person team. Q4 OKRs: ship search v2, hire 2 mid-level. Q4 review with VP in 2 weeks.

expected_output.sample:
## Q1 — Urgent + Important
- **Review hiring scorecards** — 2 interviews tomorrow, real consequence if you go in cold.

## Q2 — Important + Not Urgent
- **Q4 planning offsite prep** — 2 weeks out but the offsite is the leverage point for the whole quarter.
- **Fix the deploy script** — no deadline, but it's slowing every deploy by 10 min × 5 deploys/week × 8 people.

## Q3 — Urgent + Not Important
- **Reply to 47 emails** — batch into a 45-min block; delegate any that should go to your EA.

## Q4 — Drop
- **Update LinkedIn profile** — not driving any current outcome.

## 🪞 Masking patterns
- 'Reply to 47 emails' is masking the fact that you've been avoiding the hiring scorecard review (which felt heavier). Sorting email feels like work.

use_cases.example: Dump your inbox + to-do app + post-its into the prompt; get a clean Q1-Q4 plan for the week.

use_cases.scenario: Pre-1:1 prep with a direct report
use_cases.example: Have your report dump their list before the 1:1; the matrix becomes the conversation guide.
Sort your 'someday/maybe' list; everything that's still in Q4 after 90 days gets archived.
Treat next week's recurring meetings as 'tasks'. The Q4 column reveals meetings you should drop.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for genuinely complex projects with many sub-tasks — the matrix flattens hierarchy. Break the project into sub-tasks first. Also useless if your context is incomplete (e.g., the model doesn't know which deadlines are real).

few_shot_examples.input:
tasks: ['Write blog post on new feature', 'Update team OKRs (overdue 2 weeks)', 'Reply to investor intro email (8 hours old)', 'Schedule physical exam', 'Quarterly performance reviews (due in 2 days)', 'Refactor old onboarding code', 'Slack engineering channel about deploy etiquette']
context: 'CTO at Series-A startup, 12 eng team, fundraising in 2 months'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Q1 — Urgent + Important
- **Quarterly performance reviews** — hard deadline 2 days, direct impact on retention.
- **Reply to investor intro email** — fundraising window matters; 24h response time is the norm.

## Q2 — Important + Not Urgent
- **Update team OKRs** — overdue 2 weeks; the longer it slips the more team drifts.
- **Write blog post on new feature** — marketing leverage for the fundraise.

## Q3 — Urgent + Not Important
- **Slack about deploy etiquette** — feels urgent but it's a 5-min message; batch into your next Slack-block.

## Q4 — Drop
- **Refactor old onboarding code** — no current goal points to it. Re-evaluate after fundraise.
- **Schedule physical exam** — important for you personally but not work-prioritization scope; move to personal list.

## 🪞 Masking patterns
- The blog post is sitting in Q2 but it's been there 3 weeks. That's a Q2 → Q1 escalation signal — you've been avoiding it. Block 90 minutes Monday morning before email.

model_compatibility.notes: Best for the urgency-skepticism — won't auto-mark everything urgent.
Slightly more lenient on urgency; counterbalance by adding 'Be skeptical of urgency' to context.
Cheap + fast for repeated daily sorts.

variations.label: ICE/RICE scoring instead
variations.description: Use Impact × Confidence × Ease scoring for product prioritization.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace the urgent×important matrix with ICE scoring (each task: impact 1-10, confidence 1-10, ease 1-10, score=I×C×E). Output sorted list with reasoning per axis.

variations.label: Time-budgeted mode
variations.description: User has N hours this week; prompt fills time-budget by quadrant.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add input `hours_available` (e.g., 30). Output a time-budget: 'Q1: 8h, Q2: 14h, Q3: 6h batched, Q4: 0h.' Justify the Q2 allocation if it's <40% of total.

variations.label: No-coach mode
variations.description: Strip the masking-patterns section.
variations.prompt_snippet: Remove the 'MASKING CHECK' step from PROCEDURE and the '🪞 Masking patterns' output section. Useful when the user wants pure sorting.

failure_modes.symptom: Marks everything Q1 when context lacks deadlines
failure_modes.fix: provide context with at least one explicit hard deadline so the model has an anchor

failure_modes.symptom: Drops genuinely important Q4-looking items (e.g., personal health)
failure_modes.fix: instruct 'When in doubt, move to Q2 not Q4.'

failure_modes.symptom: Masking-pattern detection produces false positives that feel preachy
failure_modes.fix: cap masking observations at 2 per run; only surface if the pattern is concrete
explicit rule in PROCEDURE — 'self-imposed deadlines don't count for urgency'

faq.question: What if Q2 is huge?
faq.answer: That's actually the point of the matrix — Q2 is the highest-leverage quadrant. If Q2 has 10 items, you need to schedule blocks, not delegate them away.

faq.question: How often should I re-sort?
faq.answer: Weekly for full lists; daily for 'today's tasks' which is just a 5-item version. Re-sorting more often than daily is a procrastination signal.

faq.question: What if I disagree with the model's sorting?
faq.answer: Override it — the model has no skin in your day. The value is the questions it forces you to ask, not the answers.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library
meta_title: Eisenhower Matrix Prompt — Sort Tasks by Urgency × Importance
meta_description: Sort your task list into 4 quadrants with reasoning per task. Flags 'masking' tasks (busy-work standing in for harder work). Skeptical of urgency by default.

title: Daily Standup Update Formatter (Yesterday / Today / Blockers)
tldr: Convert raw EOD/EOM notes into a clean, 90-second standup update. Cuts filler, surfaces real blockers, and matches your team's preferred format.
full_prompt:
You write standup updates that the rest of the team will actually read. Default format: Yesterday / Today / Blockers. 3 bullets per section, hard cap.

INPUTS:
- raw_notes: free-form notes about what you did + plan to do + what's stuck
- team_format (optional): override the default Y/T/B with your team's variant (e.g., 'Shipped / Working on / Need help with')
- audience_size (optional): if >10, be tighter; if <5, can include more nuance

RULES:
- 3 bullets per section, hard cap. If you have 5 things, pick the 3 that matter to the audience.
- Each bullet 1 line. If you can't say it in 1 line, you're conflating two things.
- 'Blockers' means OTHERS need to act. If only you can unblock you, it goes in 'Today' as a task.
- Don't pad. If you have 0 blockers, say '- none.' Don't invent friction.
- No filler phrases ('just wanted to update', 'making progress on', 'getting ready to').

OUTPUT FORMAT:

**Yesterday**
- bullet
- bullet
- bullet

**Today**
- bullet
- bullet
- bullet

**Blockers**
- bullet (with @ mention of who needs to act if known)

Begin.

input_variables.name: raw_notes
input_variables.example: Finished the auth PR review for Alice. Wrote the design doc for caching, sent to team. Tried to debug the flaky test for an hour, no progress. Today: continue debugging, also have the 1:1 with Bob. Need DB credentials for staging, asked Sam yesterday no response.

input_variables.name: team_format
input_variables.example: Shipped / Working / Need help

expected_output.sample:
**Yesterday**
- Reviewed Alice's auth PR — merged with 2 minor comments.
- Caching design doc sent to team for async review (link).
- Spent 1h on the flaky billing test; no repro yet.

**Today**
- Continue the flaky test investigation (instrumenting CI).
- 1:1 with Bob (career-conv slot).
- Review the 2 caching-doc comments if they come in.

**Blockers**
- @sam — still need staging DB creds (asked Tuesday).

use_cases.example: Engineer pastes raw notes; bot formats and posts to #standup. Whole team reads in 90 seconds.
Use this prompt at end of week; merge 5 daily standups into a weekly summary by feeding all 5 in.
New hires often over-share or under-share in standup. This prompt is their starting template for the first 30 days.
EOD update in the prompt; teammate in the other timezone reads it as their morning context.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for incident reports or post-mortems — those need a different structure. Also skip when standup is verbal (the polish disappears in speech anyway).

few_shot_examples.input: raw_notes: 'spent half the day in a meeting hell, kicked off the design doc but didnt finish. fought a typescript build issue for an hour, fixed it. have the eng all-hands today + need to finish the design doc + also need someone to review my PR from monday'
few_shot_examples.output:
**Yesterday**
- Drafted the search-rewrite design doc (60% done).
- Fixed a typescript build error blocking the auth branch.
- (Most of the day was meeting-heavy — light on shipped output.)

**Today**
- Finish the design doc, send for review by EOD.
- Eng all-hands at 2pm.

**Blockers**
- Need a reviewer on my Monday PR (link).

model_compatibility.notes: Best at cutting filler without losing signal.
Recommended for production — fast, cheap, format-stable.
Tends to over-format with sub-bullets; instruct 'no sub-bullets' explicitly.

variations.label: Weekly version
variations.description: Roll up 5 days into a weekly update.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUTS: instead of daily, take 5 daily_notes. OUTPUT sections: 'This week: shipped' / 'Next week: focus' / 'Open blockers'. Cap 5 bullets per section.

variations.label: Slack-emoji mode
variations.description: Add status emojis your team uses.
variations.prompt_snippet: Prefix bullets with team-specific emojis: ✅ shipped, 🚧 in progress, 🛑 blocked. Be consistent within a section.

variations.label: Manager-summary mode
variations.description: For when you're consolidating 8 reports' standups into 1.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUTS: list of {person, raw_notes}. OUTPUT: aggregated team standup with 'Wins (across team)', 'In-flight', 'Cross-team blockers' sections; each bullet credits the relevant person.

failure_modes.symptom: Includes filler bullets to hit '3 bullets per section'
failure_modes.fix: hard rule 'less than 3 is fine, padding is forbidden.'

failure_modes.symptom: Treats personal blockers as team blockers
failure_modes.fix: definition in RULES — 'blocker = someone else must act.'
explicit 'no sub-bullets' rule
instruct 'Frame the day in terms of outcomes, not feelings.'

faq.question: How long should my raw notes be?
faq.answer: 30 seconds of typing. If you're writing 5 paragraphs, you're using the wrong tool.

faq.question: Can I run this on someone else's notes?
faq.answer: Yes, but the result will read in their voice; if you're consolidating reports' standups, use the Manager-summary variant explicitly.

faq.question: What if my team uses a non-standard format?
faq.answer: Pass it as `team_format`. The prompt adapts the section names and rules accordingly.

attribution: OSS AI Hub Prompt Library

title: Literature Review Synthesizer (claim + evidence + gap)
tldr: Synthesize 5-30 papers into a literature review with claim-evidence-gap structure, conflicting findings flagged, and an explicit 'what we don't know yet' section.
full_prompt:
You synthesize academic literature for researchers. Be rigorous about claim → evidence → gap structure; surface conflicting findings explicitly; don't paper over uncertainty.

INPUTS:
- papers: list of {citation, abstract, key_findings, methodology, sample_size, year, doi (optional)}
- research_question: the question this review answers
- scope_filters (optional): year range, methodology types, sample types

PROCEDURE:
1. For each paper, extract: central claim, evidence type (RCT/observational/meta-analysis/theoretical), strength of evidence (strong/moderate/weak based on sample + methodology).
2. Cluster papers into 3-6 thematic claims relevant to research_question.
3. For each theme: list supporting papers, conflicting papers (if any), and the gap (what neither side has answered).
4. Rank themes by evidence strength + relevance to research_question.
5. Surface 'orphan findings' — interesting results from a single paper that don't fit themes but matter.
6. End with 'What we don't know yet' — 3-5 explicit unanswered questions, each tied to a specific limitation in the existing literature.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown): Research question · 3-6 thematic claims (each: synthesis + evidence + gap) · Conflicting findings · Orphan findings · Unanswered questions · Methodology note (search strategy).

CITATION FORMAT: (Author year, paper #N from input). Don't invent citations.

Begin.

input_variables.name: papers
input_variables.example: [{citation:'Smith 2023', abstract:'...', key_findings:['X increases Y by 30%'], methodology:'RCT n=240', sample_size:240, year:2023}]

input_variables.name: research_question
input_variables.example: Does spaced repetition improve long-term retention in adult learners?

input_variables.name: scope_filters
input_variables.example: {year_range:'2018-2025', methodology_types:['RCT','meta-analysis'], sample_min:100}

expected_output.sample:
## Research question
Does spaced repetition improve long-term retention in adult learners?

## Theme 1: Spaced retrieval beats massed practice for declarative knowledge
Supported by 7 papers (5 RCT, 2 meta-analyses). Effect size d=0.45-0.78 across studies.
- Smith 2023 (RCT, n=240): +30% retention at 30 days
- Jones 2022 (meta, k=18): d=0.65 [0.52, 0.78]
**Gap:** All studies measure 30-day retention; none measure 1-year+

## Theme 2: Effect size depends on initial encoding quality
[..]

## Conflicting findings
- Wong 2024 found NO effect at small sample (n=42). Likely underpowered, but worth noting.

## What we don't know yet
1. Does the effect persist past 6 months? No studies in the 6mo-2yr window.
2. Does it work for procedural skills? All studies measure declarative.
3. Optimal spacing interval for adults >50? All samples skew 18-30.

use_cases.example: Student has 30 papers in Zotero; feeds metadata + key findings; gets the v1 chapter.
Analyst writing a market report on 'AI in healthcare'; synthesizes 20 vendor case studies + 10 academic papers.
Need the 'state of the field' section quickly; this prompt produces the structure to refine.
Before running a quantitative meta-analysis, this prompt surfaces which themes have enough studies to pool.

when_not_to_use: Don't use as substitute for reading papers — the prompt synthesizes what you tell it. Bad inputs → bad output. Also skip for fields where 'consensus' shifts often (active controversies).

few_shot_examples.input: Research question: 'Does intermittent fasting improve metabolic markers?' 5 papers: 3 RCT (positive), 1 meta (mixed), 1 observational (negative).
few_shot_examples.output:
## Theme 1: Short-term metabolic improvement (12 weeks)
All 3 RCTs showed improvements in fasting glucose and HDL. Effect size moderate.
**Gap:** None measured beyond 12 weeks.

## Conflicting findings
Observational study (Lee 2024, n=8000) found null effect. Likely confounded by self-selection — IF adherers may differ on lifestyle factors.

## What we don't know yet
1. Does the effect persist past 12 weeks (likely adaptation)?
2. Does it work without caloric restriction (3/3 RCTs included caloric deficit)?
3. Long-term cardiovascular events — no studies have follow-up >1 year.

model_compatibility.notes: Sometimes papers over the gap section.

variations.label: Conflicting-only mode
variations.description: Show only where studies disagree.
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip theme synthesis. Output only conflicting-findings section + analysis of why studies disagree (methodology, sample, definition).

variations.label: Methodology-focused
variations.description: Group by study type.
variations.prompt_snippet: Cluster by methodology (RCT vs observational vs meta-analysis). Compare across — RCTs say X, observational says Y; what does the asymmetry suggest?

variations.label: Quick-summary mode
variations.description: 1-paragraph summary, no themes.
variations.prompt_snippet: Output: 1-paragraph synthesis (3-5 sentences) + bullet list of 3 unanswered questions. For executive readers.

failure_modes.fix: Strict rule: only cite papers from the input list, by paper # if needed

failure_modes.symptom: Glosses over conflicting findings ('most studies agree...')
failure_modes.fix: Conflicting findings get their own section; never bury them
Gaps must be specific: 'No study measures past 12 weeks' not 'long-term unclear'

failure_modes.symptom: Treats all studies as equal weight
failure_modes.fix: Evidence strength column required; weak studies don't overrule strong studies in the synthesis

faq.question: How many papers minimum?
faq.answer: 5 is the floor for thematic clustering. <5 = just summarize each individually.

faq.question: How do I extract key_findings from papers?
faq.answer: Paste the abstract + your reading notes. Don't paste full papers — context wastes.

faq.question: Can it find papers for me?
faq.answer: No — this is synthesis only. Use a separate retrieval step (Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar) first.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Literature Review Synthesizer — Claim, Evidence, Gap
meta_description: Synthesize 5-30 papers into themed review with conflicting findings flagged and explicit 'what we don't know yet' section.

title: User-Interview Guide Builder
tldr: Build a 45-min user-interview guide with warm-up, core questions (open + follow-up), task scenarios, and exit. Calibrated to research question + participant type.
full_prompt:
You build interview guides for qualitative user research. Good guides extract real signal in 45 minutes; bad guides extract opinions on hypothetical features.

INPUTS:
- research_question: what you want to learn
- participant_profile: who you're talking to (role, context, why they're relevant)
- duration_minutes: 30 / 45 / 60 (default 45)
- interview_mode: in-person | video | phone (affects question types)
- hypothesis_to_test (optional): specific belief you want to challenge

STRUCTURE (45 min):
1. WARM-UP (5 min): low-stakes context questions; build rapport. NOT 'tell me about yourself' — too vague.
2. CONTEXT (10 min): the participant's day/workflow/situation relevant to the research_question. Specifics, not generalizations.
3. PROBE (15 min): the moments where research_question applies. Use 'tell me about the last time...' framing.
4. PROVOCATION (10 min): optional artifact/scenario/concept to react to. NOT a feature pitch.
5. EXIT (5 min): what they wanted to tell you but you didn't ask; permission to follow up.

QUESTION CRAFT RULES:
- Open questions only ('how', 'what', 'tell me about'), no closed yes/no.
- Anchor in specific past behavior ('the last time X happened'), not hypotheticals ('would you ever X').
- Each main question has 2-3 follow-up probes ('and then?', 'what happened next?', 'why did you decide that?').
- No leading questions ('don't you think X would be useful?').
- One question per turn; no compound ('how do you X and why?').

OUTPUT: markdown with sections + question numbering + estimated time per section + 'reminders for the interviewer' (anti-bias notes).

Begin.

input_variables.name: research_question
input_variables.example: How do engineering managers decide which dev tools to adopt for their team?

input_variables.name: participant_profile
input_variables.example: Engineering managers at 50-500 person SaaS companies who have adopted 2+ new dev tools in the past 6 months.

input_variables.name: interview_mode
input_variables.example: video

input_variables.name: hypothesis_to_test
input_variables.example: EMs decide based on team request rather than independent research

expected_output.sample:
## Warm-up (5 min)
1. Walk me through how this past week has looked for you so far.
   - Probe: anything that took more time than you expected?

## Context (10 min)
2. Tell me about the dev tools your team uses day-to-day. Which ones do you not think about anymore vs the ones that are still 'new'?
   - Probe: how did the 'old' ones get there?
   - Probe: what about the 'new' ones — what's new about them?

## Probe (15 min)
3. Tell me about the last time your team adopted a new dev tool. Walk me through how that happened, from the first time someone mentioned it to the point where it was in production.
   - Probe: who first brought it up?
   - Probe: what happened between then and the decision?
   - Probe: did anyone push back?

## Reminders for the interviewer
- Don't pitch your own product
- Silence is OK — let them fill it
- 'Why' too many times = interrogation; rotate with 'tell me more', 'and then?'

use_cases.example: Team building a new product; this prompt generates the guide for 15 customer-discovery interviews.
Past customers who left; guide focuses on the decision moment + alternatives considered.
Guide probes value perception without pitching specific prices.
Junior writes their own guide, then runs this prompt; diff is the learning.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for quantitative surveys (different prompt). Also skip for highly compliance-bound contexts where every question needs IRB review.

few_shot_examples.input: RQ: 'Why do indie devs choose Substack over Buttondown for newsletters?'. Participant: 'indie devs with 100-10000 subscribers'. Duration: 30. Mode: video.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Warm-up (3 min)
1. How's your newsletter going lately?

## Context (7 min)
2. Walk me through the last newsletter you sent — from idea to send.
   - Probe: what tools did you touch?

## Probe (15 min)
3. Tell me about choosing your newsletter platform originally. What made you pick the one you use?
   - Probe: what else did you consider?
   - Probe: was there a specific moment when you decided?
4. Have you ever considered switching? Tell me about that moment.
   - Probe: what made you stay (or switch)?

## Exit (5 min)
5. What didn't I ask that I should have?

model_compatibility.notes: Sometimes injects hypothetical 'would you...' questions; reinforce the rule.
Preferred for nuanced research (e.g., emotional / political topics).
Workable; expect to revise 2-3 questions per guide.

variations.label: Diary-study mode
variations.description: Multi-day async questions instead of single interview.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace 45-min structure with: 5 daily prompts over 7 days, each asking participant to record one specific moment relevant to research_question.

variations.label: Group / focus-group
variations.description: 6-person focus group, 90 min.
variations.prompt_snippet: Adjust for group dynamics: include 'go around the room' moments, surface disagreement intentionally, less 1:1 probing.

variations.label: Field-observation guide
variations.description: Ride-along / shadowing.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace interview questions with observation prompts: 'What does the participant do without being asked?', 'Where do they get stuck?', 'What workarounds appear?'

failure_modes.symptom: Leading questions ('Don't you think X would be helpful?')
failure_modes.fix: Forbid in question craft rules; reject leading phrasings
Anchor every probe in 'the last time' or 'a specific instance', never 'would you ever'
Open-only rule; transform 'do you X?' to 'tell me about how you X'

failure_modes.symptom: Over-scripted, no room for emergent topics
failure_modes.fix: Time-box sections; explicitly tell interviewer 'follow the participant if they go somewhere unexpected'

faq.question: How many interviews do I need?
faq.answer: 5-8 for early discovery (themes saturate fast). 12-20 for product validation. >20 = consider survey for breadth.

faq.question: How long is too long?
faq.answer: 45 min is the sweet spot. 60+ min = participants degrade. 30 min = enough for focused topics.

faq.question: Should I record?
faq.answer: Yes — with consent. Transcription + AI summary saves 5x synthesis time. But recording can affect what participants share; mention recording at warm-up.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: User Interview Guide Builder — 45-Min Structured
meta_description: Build interview guides with warm-up, context, probe, provocation, exit. Past-behavior anchors, anti-bias rules built in.

title: Competitive Teardown (feature + positioning + pricing)
tldr: Deep-dive on a single competitor: product surfaces, positioning claims, pricing tiers, sales motion, recent changes. Builds the dossier you'd need to compete.
full_prompt:
You build a competitive teardown — a single-competitor dossier deep enough to brief a new salesperson. Be evidence-based; flag where you're inferring vs citing.

INPUTS:
- competitor: {name, url, founding_year, est_revenue, est_employees, funding_stage}
- evidence: list of {source, date, content_summary} from website / docs / blog / G2 reviews / customer-call notes
- our_company_context: how we compete (positioning + segment overlap)

STRUCTURE (each section: claim + evidence + confidence):
1. POSITIONING: their elevator pitch (in their words). Who they target. What they avoid.
2. PRODUCT SURFACES: top 5-7 features they emphasize publicly. What's MISSING that we'd expect.
3. PRICING & PACKAGING: tier structure, anchor prices, free tier (if any), enterprise gates.
4. SALES MOTION: PLG / sales-led / hybrid. Trial vs demo. Self-serve vs gated.
5. STRENGTHS (against us): 3-5 areas where they have real advantage.
6. WEAKNESSES (we should attack): 3-5 areas where we win.
7. RECENT MOMENTUM: hiring, launches, fundraise, customer-logo growth (last 6 months).
8. CUSTOMER VOICE: 3-5 representative quotes from reviews — balanced positive/negative.

EVIDENCE RULES:
- Every claim cites the input source by index.
- Inferences are tagged 'INFERRED' with confidence (low/med/high).
- Never fabricate quotes or stats.

Begin.

input_variables.name: competitor
input_variables.example: {name:'Acme Analytics', url:'acme.com', founding_year:2019, est_revenue:'$15M ARR', est_employees:75, funding_stage:'Series B'}

input_variables.name: evidence
input_variables.description: Source material — website, docs, reviews, calls
input_variables.example: [{source:'acme.com homepage', date:'2026-05-13', content_summary:'Headline: ...'}]

input_variables.name: our_company_context
input_variables.example: We target the same mid-market segment with usage-based pricing vs their seat-based.

expected_output.sample:
## 1. POSITIONING
**Claim:** 'The analytics platform built for product teams who hate dashboards.' [source #1]
**Target:** PMs and growth at Series-B+ SaaS [#1, #4]
**Avoid:** Enterprise BI use case — explicitly steered away from in pricing page FAQ [#2]
**Confidence:** high

## 5. STRENGTHS (against us)
1. Native Salesforce sync [#3] — we don't have this. (high)
2. INFERRED: faster onboarding (1-line setup per docs) [#5, INFERRED med]
3. Stronger PM-community presence (3 conferences sponsored in 6 months) [#7]

## 6. WEAKNESSES (we should attack)
1. No usage-based pricing — costs scale linearly with seats [#2]
2. No self-serve enterprise tier; gated by sales [#2]
3. G2 reviews flag slow support response (3/5 negative quotes mention) [#8]

## CUSTOMER VOICE
> 'Game-changer for our PM team. Setup was 10 minutes.' (G2 reviewer, May 2026) [#8]
> 'Pricing scales painfully — we added 10 seats and bill jumped $4k/mo.' (G2 reviewer, April 2026) [#8]

use_cases.example: New AE needs to know top 3 competitors; this prompt produces the dossier for each.
Sales-enablement extracts the strengths/weaknesses sections into a 1-pager battle card.
Investor due-diligence requires competitive landscape; teardowns of top 3 competitors anchor the response.
Quarterly re-run with refreshed evidence; momentum section highlights what's changed.

when_not_to_use: Don't use without real evidence input — the prompt won't fabricate intel. Also skip for stealth competitors (no public surface to scrape).

few_shot_examples.input: Competitor: vector-db company. Evidence: 3 sources (homepage, pricing page, G2 reviews). Our context: open-source self-hostable.
few_shot_examples.output:
## 1. POSITIONING
'Production-ready vector search at enterprise scale.' Targets ML teams at >500-person companies [#1]. Avoids hobbyist use case [INFERRED med — no community/free tier prominently shown].

## 3. PRICING & PACKAGING
Free dev tier (1M vectors). Pro $99/mo (10M vectors). Enterprise 'contact us' [#2].

## 6. WEAKNESSES (we should attack)
1. Closed-source — self-host requires enterprise tier [#2]
2. G2 reviews flag lock-in concern: 'data export is JSON-only, slow' [#3]

model_compatibility.notes: Strong structure; sometimes weakens the 'INFERRED' tagging.
Workable; verify every claim against evidence input.

variations.label: Pricing-only teardown
variations.description: Deep on packaging.
variations.prompt_snippet: Focus all sections on pricing/packaging. Add 'price-per-value' analysis (cost per 1k requests, per seat, per anything) for cross-tier comparison.

variations.label: Sales-call-style
variations.description: Tone shifts to 'how to handle in sales calls'.
variations.prompt_snippet: Reframe strengths/weaknesses as 'objection-handling cheat-sheet': for each competitor strength, the line our AE should use; for each weakness, the question that exposes it.

variations.label: Multi-competitor batch
variations.description: Loop over 3-5 competitors.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT becomes list of competitors. Output per-competitor teardown + cross-competitor comparison table at the end.

failure_modes.symptom: Fabricates quotes from G2 / customer calls
failure_modes.fix: Quotes must come from evidence input, with source index; otherwise mark 'no customer voice in inputs'
INFERRED tag mandatory; confidence rating per inferred claim
Each must reference a specific evidence point; vague claims rejected

failure_modes.symptom: Outdated info passed off as current
failure_modes.fix: Surface evidence date per claim; flag any claim older than 6 months as 'stale, refresh'

faq.question: How fresh should evidence be?
faq.answer: <6 months for product/pricing; <3 months for momentum signals. Stale evidence misleads.

faq.question: Where do I source evidence?
faq.answer: Homepage, pricing page, docs, G2/Capterra reviews, Glassdoor (sales-motion hints), LinkedIn (hiring), Wellfound (engineering blog).

faq.question: Can it scrape the competitor's site?
faq.answer: No — it synthesizes evidence you provide. Pair with a scraper if you need automation.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Competitive Teardown — Evidence-Based Dossier
meta_description: Single-competitor deep dive: positioning, pricing, sales motion, strengths/weaknesses, customer voice. Evidence-cited, inference-tagged.

title: Market Sizing via Fermi Estimation
tldr: Build a TAM/SAM/SOM estimate via explicit Fermi chains. Each assumption is named, sourced (or flagged as estimate), and sensitivity-tested.
full_prompt:
You build market-size estimates using Fermi-style chains. The output looks rigorous because every assumption is named — not because you used pretty numbers.

INPUTS:
- product: 1-paragraph description of what's being sold
- buyer_definition: who the buyer is (role, company size, geography)
- price_point: what they'd pay (annual or one-time)
- known_anchors: list of {claim, source} for anchors (e.g., '300k US engineering managers', BLS 2024)

PROCEDURE:
1. TAM (Total Addressable Market) — every buyer who could plausibly use this. Use Fermi chain: starting universe × narrowing factor × narrowing factor → buyers; × price → $ TAM.
2. SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) — TAM restricted to geographies/segments you actually serve.
3. SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market) — SAM × realistic share you can capture in N years.
4. For every multiplication, name the source: cited / industry estimate / your assumption.
5. Sensitivity test: what's the TAM if each assumption shifts ±20%? Flag the assumption that matters most.
6. Add a 'reality check' section: similar companies and what they actually captured, as a sanity check.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with TAM chain (each line: 'X × Y = Z, source: ...'), SAM, SOM, sensitivity table, reality check.

Begin.

input_variables.name: product
input_variables.example: Self-hosted analytics for SaaS companies, drop-in alternative to PostHog/Mixpanel

input_variables.name: buyer_definition
input_variables.example: Engineering lead at a SaaS company with 10-500 employees in North America + Europe

input_variables.name: price_point
input_variables.description: Annual contract value or one-time price
input_variables.example: $3,000 / year average

input_variables.name: known_anchors
input_variables.example: [{claim:'30,000 SaaS companies in NA+EU at 10-500 employees', source:'Crunchbase 2025 SaaS report'}]

expected_output.sample:
## TAM (Fermi chain)
- 30,000 SaaS companies in NA+EU (10-500 employees) — Crunchbase 2025
- × 70% have at least 1 product/analytics tool in budget — industry estimate
- = 21,000 buyers
- × $3,000 ACV = **$63M TAM**

## SAM
- Restrict to English-speaking + SOC2-ready = 75% of TAM
- = **$47M SAM**

## SOM (3-year)
- Realistic share: 2% (your assumption; sanity-check: PostHog at year-3 captured ~3% of TAM)
- = **$940k SOM by year 3**

## Sensitivity (±20%)
| Assumption | Low | Base | High |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buyer count | 16,800 | 21,000 | 25,200 |
| ACV | $2,400 | $3,000 | $3,600 |
| Share % | 1.6% | 2% | 2.4% |
| → SOM | $645k | $940k | $1,361k |

The ACV is the most-sensitive lever — small ACV changes move SOM more than buyer count.

## Reality check
- PostHog: $15M ARR at year 5 (~$70M TAM × 21% share — likely they're now expanding TAM definition).
- Plausible: matches the order of magnitude.

use_cases.example: Founder needs the TAM slide; this prompt builds the chain that holds up to investor scrutiny.
Internal: 'How big can this product line be?' — chain reveals if the answer justifies investment.
Re-run with different geo restrictions to compare expansion priorities.
Sensitivity table shows whether ACV move from $3k → $6k materially changes SOM.

when_not_to_use: Don't use as the only basis for an investment decision — Fermi is order-of-magnitude, not precision. Also skip for very early markets (no comparable anchors).

few_shot_examples.input: Product: 'AI-powered legal contract review for SMBs'. Buyer: 'GC or office manager at US companies 50-500 employees'. Price: $5k/yr. Anchors: '120k US companies in that range'.
few_shot_examples.output:
## TAM
- 120,000 US companies (50-500 employees) — Census 2024
- × 60% have legal/compliance budget — industry estimate
- = 72,000 buyers
- × $5,000 ACV = **$360M TAM**

## SAM
- Restrict to companies using e-signature (proxy for digital-doc maturity) = 80%
- = **$288M SAM**

## SOM (3-year)
- 1.5% share (legal tech adoption is slow)
- = **$4.3M SOM**

## Sensitivity
Most sensitive: budget penetration (60%) — if actually 40%, TAM drops to $240M.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at distinguishing cited vs estimated anchors.
Reliable arithmetic; sometimes invents anchor sources.

variations.label: Top-down version
variations.description: Start from total industry spend and slice down.
variations.prompt_snippet: Override Fermi chain: start from industry-spend total (e.g., '$5B US legal-tech spend'), narrow by segment, then by product type.

variations.label: Bottoms-up version
variations.description: Start from per-customer detail and multiply up.
variations.prompt_snippet: Build from typical customer: one team buys, what they pay, multiply by buyer count. Reveals unit economics vs total-pie framing.

variations.label: Geographic breakout
variations.description: Per-region SOM.
variations.prompt_snippet: Split SAM and SOM by region (NA / EU / APAC / LATAM). Surface which region is most underserved relative to TAM.

failure_modes.fix: Strict rule: sources must come from known_anchors input or be tagged 'estimate'

failure_modes.symptom: Multiplies through several speculative assumptions to reach a pretty number
failure_modes.fix: Every assumption requires explicit confidence (cited/industry-estimate/our-assumption); the sensitivity table surfaces the cost
Mandatory section — list at least 2 comparable companies and what they actually captured

failure_modes.symptom: Confuses TAM with revenue potential year 1
failure_modes.fix: TAM/SAM/SOM/Year-1 are distinct; always clarify the time horizon for SOM

faq.question: Investor doesn't believe the TAM number?
faq.answer: Walk them through the chain. Every assumption is named; they can challenge any one. The sensitivity table shows you've already thought about it.

faq.question: What's a realistic 3-year SOM?
faq.answer: Depends on category maturity. New categories: 0.5-2%. Established: 2-5%. Replacement plays: 1-3%. Don't claim >10% in 3 years unless you have evidence.

faq.question: Should I use top-down or bottom-up?
faq.answer: Both. They should converge within ±2x; if they don't, one is wrong.

attribution: OSS AI Hub
meta_title: Market Sizing via Fermi — TAM/SAM/SOM with Sensitivity
meta_description: Build TAM/SAM/SOM with explicit Fermi chains, sourced assumptions, and sensitivity analysis. Survives investor scrutiny.

title: Regex Builder from Examples
content:
You are an expert in code generation. Task: Given positive and negative match examples, produce a tested regex with explanation of each group. Outputs in PCRE + JavaScript flavors.

Inputs: {inputs}

Requirements:
- Be specific and actionable, not generic.
- Cite evidence or reasoning for non-obvious claims.
- Output in a clean, structured format (use headers / bullets / code blocks as appropriate).
- If you lack information, say so explicitly rather than guessing.

Add 'Cite source or say I don't know' to instructions.

faq.question: Does this work on smaller open models?
faq.answer: Yes for Llama 3.3 70B / Qwen2.5 72B / Mixtral; quality drops noticeably below 30B.

faq.answer: Yes - 2-3 few-shot examples almost always lift performance, especially on smaller models.
